structure.title: Document Purpose
structure.summary: The partial document outlines the filled service-record pack for AIM IT Park Sampling, part of the AIM Entity Brain knowledge-ingestion framework. It explains that this document is a standalone child record under the parent service “Product Sampling,” allowing for independent review and validation of parent-child inheritance. The main content defines IT Park Sampling as a targeted product sampling service aimed at professionals within office or tech parks, providing structured exposure to products in workplace environments. It details what the service includes—such as touchpoint recommendations, sampling execution, product education options, campaign reporting, and basic feedback mechanisms—and clarifies exclusions like guaranteed park access, database capture, or advanced fabrication. The document also specifies when this child service should be recommended, namely when targeting working professionals, office-goers, and urban salaried audiences.

structure.title: Service Record Header
structure.summary: The partial document outlines the “AIM IT Park Sampling — Filled Service-Record Pack,” a child service record under AIM’s Product Sampling framework. It explains the document’s purpose as an independently reviewable service record for IT Park Sampling, distinct from the main PRD and parent pack to allow isolated validation and iteration. The service definition describes IT Park Sampling as a targeted sampling activity conducted within tech or business parks to engage working professionals and urban office-goers. It lists included activities such as location recommendations, audience-tier selection, promoter or kiosk-led sampling, reporting, and optional feedback or education. Exclusions include guaranteed corporate access, employee data, sales attribution, premium kiosks, or heavy compliance unless scoped. The document clarifies that IT Park Sampling should be chosen when Product Sampling fits and the core audience comprises urban, salaried professionals or premium employee groups.

structure.title: Service Definition
structure.summary: The partial document is a filled service-record pack for AIM’s IT Park Sampling service under the parent service Product Sampling. It explains that the purpose of this standalone document is to review the specific service independently, verify parent-child inheritance, and enable iteration without affecting larger documents. The service definition describes IT Park Sampling as a targeted product sampling activity conducted in office and tech parks to engage working professionals and urban employees. It lists included deliverables such as touchpoint recommendations, audience-tier selection, promoter or kiosk sampling, product education options, campaign reporting, and optional feedback mechanisms. Exclusions are also outlined, including no guaranteed access to all corporate parks, no assured employee data capture, or guaranteed conversions, and no premium fabrication or full enterprise compliance unless scoped. The document concludes by defining when this service should be recommended—specifically for campaigns aimed at working professionals and premium urban employee segments.

structure.title: Child-Service Purpose and Role Under Product Sampling
structure.summary: The partial document describes the AIM IT Park Sampling — Filled Service-Record Pack, which details a specific child service under AIM’s Product Sampling framework. It explains the purpose of separating this service record for independent review, validation of parent-child inheritance, and iterative refinement. The document defines IT Park Sampling as a targeted product sampling service conducted within office or tech parks to engage working professionals and urban salaried consumers. It outlines what the service includes—such as office-park targeting, sample distribution, and optional feedback mechanisms—and clarifies exclusions like guaranteed park access or deep compliance handling. The document positions this child service as most suitable when the parent Product Sampling is relevant but the audience is specifically working professionals. It lists target audiences, what the service solves, and specifies best-fit, moderate, and weak objectives according to campaign goals. Additionally, it identifies industries and product categories that align best, such as beauty, personal care, wellness, nutrition, beverages, and FMCG products intended for urban professionals.

structure.title: Best-Fit Objectives
structure.summary: The partial document focuses on marketing and sampling strategies targeting weekday consumer audiences located in business hubs, primarily working professionals. It emphasizes effective approaches for introducing products in urban, premium, or semi-premium environments during weekday routines. The main objectives outlined include generating product trials among employed decision-makers, building awareness with a precise urban audience, and educating consumers about productivity, wellness, and convenience-oriented products. It categorizes objectives by fit—strong, moderate, and weak—based on how closely they align with the office-going target group. Additionally, it identifies best-fit industries such as beauty, skincare, personal care, wellness, nutrition, beverages, premium FMCG, and health-oriented products designed for working professionals. The document highlights that this child service is particularly suited for premium or trial-based product launches aimed at professional consumers.

structure.title: Best-Fit Industry and Category Logic
structure.summary: The partial document outlines a marketing or sampling service designed for targeting weekday consumer audiences in business hubs, specifically working professionals in urban settings. It details the service’s strengths in providing product trials, awareness, and education among employed, decision-making consumers for categories such as productivity, wellness, convenience, and premium self-care. The objectives emphasize precision targeting, trial generation, and awareness building within business environments, while noting limited effectiveness for household or student-oriented campaigns. It defines best-fit industries like beauty, personal care, wellness, beverages, and premium FMCG, with ideal product traits being office-relevant, easy to sample, and suitable for professional environments. The document provides fit and misfit logic, identifying alignment with brands seeking targeted engagement with office-goers and a mismatch with broad mass or family-first targeting. Finally, it advises refining audience definition within the “working professionals” segment, distinguishing between general and premium urban professionals.

structure.title: Fit and Misfit Logic
structure.summary: The partial document outlines guidelines for determining how well a product aligns with an office or workplace-based consumer engagement strategy. It categorizes products by their degree of fit—strong, moderate, or weak—based on factors such as target audience, sampling ease, distribution feasibility, and relevance to employed urban consumers. Strong-fit products are those aimed at working professionals or premium urban audiences that can be easily sampled in weekday office environments. Moderate-fit products include general FMCG items relevant to office-goers but lacking premium positioning. Weak-fit products require household decision-making, lengthy demonstrations, or complex compliance unsuitable for office settings. It also details fit and misfit signals for brands and clients, addressing targeting precision, logistical feasibility, and appropriate environments. Finally, it introduces audience logic, emphasizing the need to refine the concept of “working professionals” by distinguishing between general office-goers and premium professionals.

structure.title: Audience Logic
structure.summary: The partial document outlines a framework for assessing product suitability and executing sampling within office or IT park environments. It defines criteria for strong, moderate, and weak fit between products and workplace-based sampling contexts, emphasizing relevance to urban, employed, and premium consumers. It details signals indicating fit or misfit based on product targeting, sampling goals, and logistical factors. Audience logic focuses on refining the definition of “working professionals” through dimensions such as gender, age, income, and product use relevance in weekday office settings. Examples illustrate appropriate audience positioning for different product types. The document also describes three primary service models for IT park sampling—promoter-led handout, kiosk/table-led sampling, and engagement-linked sampling—highlighting optimal use cases for each. Lastly, it references a discovery question bank to guide client understanding and execution decisions.

The section on core service models outlines three primary execution approaches for IT park sampling—promoter-led handouts, kiosk/table-led sampling, and engagement-linked sampling—each suited to varying levels of cost, interaction, and brand positioning. It also emphasizes a decision rule to choose sampling models based on required interaction rather than assuming a kiosk setup. Finally, it introduces a discovery question bank tailored to IT park sampling for deeper audience understanding.

It also provides guidance on handling client objections—focusing on audience relevance, managing expectations around venue access, and balancing cost versus quality. Finally, scoring cues are listed to evaluate project potential, emphasizing appropriate audience targeting, realistic scope, and client openness to recommendations.

It addresses feasibility risks such as unrealistic data capture formats, limited venue access, tight timelines, and budget mismatches. Pricing logic is explained through variables like city cluster, venue tier, execution model, manpower, and branding requirements, emphasizing that IT park opportunities should not be priced uniformly. The document provides guidance for handling client objections by focusing on audience fit and simplifying scope. Scoring cues identify positive, negative, and strategic value signals related to working-professional audiences and brand alignment. structure.title: Recommended Immediate Next Pack
structure.summary:
The partial document presents a comprehensive set of internal knowledge and operational artifacts for AIM’s “Entity Brain” system—a commercial-intelligence and recommendation platform intended to emulate founder-level decision-making in sales, discovery, and proposal processes. The content includes detailed rulebooks, playbooks, and service records. It covers the following main areas:

1. **Cross-Industry Recommendation Matrix** – Defines the system’s top-level logic for recommending AIM services across industries. It explains how to rank and prioritize services such as sampling, activations, or outreach based on industry, audience, objectives, environment sensitivity, and trust sensitivity. It introduces rules for precision-first versus scale-first decision logic, pilot-first use, hybrid campaign triggers, and suppression of low-fit options.

2. **Industry Playbook — Beauty / Skincare** – Deeply analyzes the behavior, objectives, and priorities of beauty and skincare brands. It details buyer personas, objectives, target audiences, feasible service fits, commercial risk factors, and service fit patterns. It outlines recommendation patterns for IT parks, malls, retail, and community sampling, provides scoring signals, and lists validation and readiness tasks before publication.

3. **Industry-to-Service Decision Tree + Scenario Test Library** – Operationalizes the recommendation logic through practical, conditional decision trees and scenario-based tests. It defines structured flows for selecting between sampling, activation, retail, and visibility options and includes realistic examples from multiple industries (Beauty, FMCG, Wellness, Baby Care, Pet Care, etc.). It demonstrates pilot-first logic, hybrid triggers, negative cases, and evaluation rubrics.

4. **Product Sampling (Core) — Parent Service Record** – Establishes a parent service template for all sampling-based offerings. It defines scope, fit/misfit signals, objectives, quote and feasibility inputs, pricing drivers, scoring cues, risk flags, and discovery requirements. It also sets JSON schemas and inheritance logic for child services such as IT Park, RWA, Mall, and In-Store Sampling.

5. **IT Park Sampling — Child Service Record** – Details the specialized service focused on workplace-based product trials. It defines its audience logic (working professionals), best-fit industries, objectives, execution models (promoter, kiosk, engagement), fit/misfit signals, feasibility criteria, cost drivers, objection handling, scoring cues, and case study tagging. It sets explicit validation items and points to RWA Sampling as the next artifact.

6. **AIM Entity Brain PRD (Product Requirements Document)** – Outlines the core system architecture, goals, user problems, modules, data models, workflows, and governance controls for the Entity Brain product. It emphasizes service and industry intelligence, discovery logic, solution mapping, lead scoring, proposal readiness checks, CRM synchronization, and human override governance. It defines user interfaces (Sales Copilot, Chatbot, Founder and Admin panels), field tracking standards, success metrics, rollout plan, and guardrails.

Overall, the document defines a hierarchical and data-structured intelligence framework connecting services, industries, decision logic, and validation processes to enable automated, consistent, commercially intelligent recommendations and CRM-ready outputs for AIM’s sales ecosystem.

structure.title: Phased Rollout Plan
structure.summary: The partial document outlines performance measurement metrics and a staged implementation plan for an AI-driven sales and lead management system. It describes lead quality, bot performance, and learning metrics focused on conversion rates, accuracy, and system improvements over time. It then details a four-phase rollout plan starting with creating a structured knowledge foundation, followed by developing an internal sales copilot, an external discovery bot, and finally a voice-based prospecting engine, each with specific deliverables and goals to enhance sales automation and intelligence. The section on risks identifies potential challenges such as data overload, weak discovery logic, AI overpromising, low adoption, and early scoring inaccuracies, alongside mitigation strategies. The document concludes with a section defining the MVP for initial deployment.

structure.title: Risks
structure.summary: The partial document outlines several development phases of an AI-enabled sales and discovery system. It describes internal and external components designed to improve lead qualification, discovery efficiency, and reduce founder dependency. Phase 3 focuses on building an external discovery bot using website and WhatsApp interfaces, capable of stateful questioning, automatic lead structuring, and providing human handoff summaries, with the goal of consistent lead qualification before sales involvement. Phase 4 introduces a voice prospecting engine with features such as AI-driven outbound calling, transcript intelligence, callback routing, objection capture, and a review dashboard aimed at scaling prospecting activities. The document also identifies major risks—including information overload, weak discovery logic, overpromising, low user adoption, and incorrect scoring—and proposes mitigations for each. It concludes with an MVP definition section, suggesting a focus on establishing minimal viable functionality for testing and validation.

structure.title: MVP Definition
structure.summary: The partial document outlines the design and development plan for AIM’s commercial intelligence and discovery system. It details successive project phases including CRM sync, external discovery bot, and voice prospecting engine, each with specific deliverables and goals focused on improving internal discovery, qualifying leads, and scaling prospecting through AI. The risks section identifies potential implementation challenges such as data overload, weak discovery logic, and low adoption, along with mitigation strategies. The MVP definition specifies included and excluded features, emphasizing structured knowledge, scoring, CRM integration, and founder oversight while excluding public or autonomous functions at launch. Launch recommendations propose a phased rollout prioritizing internal tools for faster ROI and safer learning. The final recommendation stresses building the system as an internal copilot before public deployment to ensure quality and reduced dependency on founders. The document also lists the next set of deliverables—functional specifications, CRM mappings, ontology, user stories, APIs, wireframes, and rollout plans—and begins the functional specification for the Knowledge Core module, describing its purpose and inputs.

structure.title: Launch Recommendation
structure.summary: The partial document outlines the scope and strategy for developing the MVP of AIM, detailing what features are included and excluded. Included features cover core and sub-services, target industries, discovery questions, recommendation logic, scoring engine, CRM integration, and internal sales copilot with founder oversight. Excluded items focus on public chatbot, voice calling, autonomous pricing, and proposal automation at launch. It recommends a specific launch sequence to ensure fast ROI and safe learning, emphasizing building AIM first as an internal commercial intelligence copilot rather than a public chatbot to enhance lead and CRM quality, generate training data, and validate logic safely. The document also lists next steps for documentation, including functional specifications, CRM mapping, ontology, user stories, API specs, wireframes, and rollout plans. A section introduces the Knowledge Core module, describing its purpose to store and manage structured commercial intelligence and its input requirements.

structure.title: Final Recommendation
structure.summary: The partial document outlines the Minimum Viable Product (MVP) for an AI-driven commercial intelligence and discovery copilot called AIM. It lists what the MVP will include—such as core services, discovery questions, scoring and recommendation systems, CRM integration, and internal sales copilot—and what it will exclude, like public chatbot and voice calling features at launch. It provides a recommended launch sequence emphasizing internal deployment first for faster ROI and safer learning. The final recommendation focuses on developing AIM as an internal tool before expanding to public-facing automation. The document also specifies the next set of documents to produce, including functional specifications, CRM mapping, ontology, user stories, and integration plans. Finally, it begins detailing the functional specification for the Knowledge Core module, defining its purpose and inputs.

nodes.title: Module: Knowledge Core
nodes.summary: The partial document outlines the scope, launch plans, and functional specifications for the Minimum Viable Product (MVP) of the AIM system. It details what the MVP will include—core services, industry targeting, curated discovery questions, service recommendation logic, scoring engine, CRM integration, internal sales copilot, and founder review features—and what it excludes, such as public chatbot, voice calling, and autonomous pricing or proposal issuance at launch. It specifies the recommended launch sequence to maximize ROI and learning, emphasizing an internal commercial intelligence copilot as the starting point before public-facing automation. The document also lists subsequent documentation to create, including functional modules, CRM mapping, ontology, user stories, APIs, and wireframes. It then provides module-level specifications for the Knowledge Core and Discovery Engine, defining each module’s purpose, inputs, core functions, outputs, business rules, dependencies, and administrative actions. The Knowledge Core focuses on managing structured commercial intelligence, while the Discovery Engine handles dynamic questioning and information completeness during lead discovery.

nodes.title: Module: Discovery Engine
nodes.summary: The partial document outlines components of a system focused on service discovery and solution mapping. It begins with administrative functions such as managing industry playbooks, editing questions, scoring logic, and uploading case study assets. The “Discovery Engine” module is described as a mechanism for running context-aware questioning and tracking the state of information during lead evaluation. It details its inputs, core functions, outputs, business rules, and dependencies, emphasizing dynamic question selection, field validation, and readiness assessment. The subsequent “Solution Mapper” module transforms discovered requirements into actionable solution recommendations, covering inputs, core functions, outputs, and business rules. It focuses on generating tailored service mixes, estimating budgets and timelines, identifying case studies, and managing feasibility and risk flags to support proposal development.

nodes.title: Module: Solution Mapper
nodes.summary:
The partial document outlines a structured system composed of several functional modules within a business intelligence and lead management framework.  
It begins with references to dependencies and core components such as the knowledge core, orchestration engine, CRM field mapping, and conversation layer.  
The **Solution Mapper module** focuses on translating discovered requirements into solution paths, taking inputs like service and industry classifications, lead attributes, rules, and pricing references. Its core functions include recommending service mixes, budget and timeline estimates, and identifying risks and case studies, while enforcing rules around feasibility and pricing approval.  
The **FitScore Engine module** evaluates and scores leads using commercial logic based on authority, fit, budget, urgency, and feasibility data. It calculates component and total scores, assigns lead bands, recommends SLA and escalation levels, and outputs score breakdowns and routing guidance, with business rules ensuring accuracy and proper prioritization.  
The **CRM Intelligence Sync module** is designed to map refined lead intelligence and scoring data into the CRM system to initiate workflows, ensuring integration between analytical results and operational systems.

nodes.title: Module: FitScore Engine
nodes.summary: The partial document outlines two modules within a larger system: the FitScore Engine and the CRM Intelligence Sync. The FitScore Engine module focuses on evaluating leads using commercial logic based on factors like authority, budget, urgency, completeness, feasibility, and strategic value. It produces component and total scores, assigns lead bands, recommends service-level agreements, and defines escalation paths. Business rules specify how missing or strong data affect scoring and prioritization. The CRM Intelligence Sync module handles the integration of processed lead intelligence into the CRM system, mapping structured data and scores to initiate workflow actions. The overall emphasis is on accurate scoring, prioritization, and seamless integration of actionable intelligence into CRM processes.

nodes.title: Module: CRM Intelligence Sync
nodes.summary:
The partial document outlines three interconnected system modules within a lead management or sales intelligence platform. 

The **FitScore Engine** module evaluates sales leads using various input factors such as authority, fit, budget, urgency, and strategic value to produce component and total scores, assign lead bands, and recommend SLA levels and escalation paths. It follows rules ensuring completeness, feasibility, and appropriately handling strategic value.

The **CRM Intelligence Sync** module is responsible for integrating lead intelligence into the CRM system by creating or updating lead records, assigning ownership, generating tasks, and logging activities. It includes validation and audit requirements to ensure data consistency and prevent duplication.

The **Sales Copilot** module assists salespeople in real-time by presenting lead insights, identifying missing data, suggesting discovery questions, and recommending tailored pitch approaches, case studies, and risk highlights. Together, these modules automate lead scoring, CRM synchronization, and sales guidance processes.

nodes.title: Module: Sales Copilot
nodes.summary: The partial document outlines several modules related to lead management and sales operations within a CRM-integrated system. It details their purposes, inputs, core functions, outputs, and associated business rules. The "Recommendations" section focuses on lead creation, updates, ownership assignment, priority tagging, and ensuring auditable CRM interactions. The "Sales Copilot" module supports salespeople during discovery and qualification, providing live guidance, field visibility, question suggestions, and proposal readiness insights, while allowing logged overrides. The "Discovery Chatbot" module facilitates conversational external prospect qualification, managing inquiries, maintaining context, summarizing requirements, and syncing data to CRM, with rules ensuring clarity, efficiency, and appropriate escalation. The beginning of the "Founder Review & Override" module is included but not elaborated.

nodes.title: Module: Discovery Chatbot
nodes.summary: The partial document outlines modules within a sales or proposal management system. It describes objectives, inputs, core functions, outputs, and business rules for two components. The first component focuses on proposal readiness, aiming to guide salespeople, enhance discovery quality, and speed up transitions to the proposal stage, with rules governing salesperson overrides and logging. The second component details a Discovery Chatbot used for structured yet conversational prospect qualification, processing incoming messages and context to generate lead briefs and summaries. It includes operational rules such as avoiding repetition, gathering only necessary details, handling scope limits, and maintaining conversation context. A third module, Founder Review & Override, is introduced but not detailed.

nodes.title: Module: Founder Review & Override
nodes.summary: The partial document describes processes and components within a sales or lead management system focused on oversight and continuous learning. It outlines a mechanism for reviewing and correcting high-value or low-confidence leads by senior personnel, specifying inputs like lead summaries, scores, and confidence statuses. Core functions include showing escalation context, allowing edits or overrides, logging reasons for changes, and feeding those corrections back into the system for learning. Outputs include a final approved decision, learning signals, and updates to CRM and recommendation states. Business rules mandate logging all overrides and categorizing corrections by type. Additionally, the section on the Learning & Evaluation Layer details how the system improves over time by analyzing outcomes, override logs, and performance metrics—tracking module performance, identifying patterns of failure, evaluating question and scoring effectiveness, and refining the system’s intelligence.

nodes.title: Module: Learning & Evaluation Layer
nodes.summary: The partial document outlines modules and user stories related to improving lead management and system learning within a sales organization. It describes a process for escalating and reviewing high-value or low-confidence leads, including inputs, core functions, outputs, and business rules for logging and categorizing overrides. The “Learning & Evaluation Layer” module focuses on continuous improvement by analyzing outcomes, overrides, and performance metrics to refine scoring and recommendation logic. The user stories define the goals and needs of different roles: sales executives seeking guidance and enriched lead context, sales heads focusing on lead prioritization and quality oversight, founders aiming to embed their reasoning into scalable systems and improve through overrides, and operations managers requiring clear handoff details and early feasibility checks. Overall, the document emphasizes structured decision review, feedback-driven learning, and role-specific functionality to enhance sales effectiveness and operational alignment.

structure.title: User Stories
structure.summary:
The partial document outlines functional specifications and user stories for a sales and lead management system built around knowledge logic, discovery, scoring, and CRM integration. It includes processes for review cycles, update recommendations, evaluation dashboards, and business rules ensuring approved logic governs production behavior while maintaining historical integrity. The user stories address multiple stakeholders—Sales Executives seeking guided discovery and proposal readiness insights, Sales Heads wanting automated lead prioritization and logic control, Founders aiming to codify commercial reasoning and oversee exceptions, Operations Managers focused on clean sales handoffs and feasibility checks, CRM Admins managing data consistency and duplicate prevention, and External Prospects expecting intelligent, context-aware interactions. The acceptance criteria define technical and functional expectations for six modules:  
- **Knowledge Core:** service versioning, field mapping, and logic archiving.  
- **Discovery Engine:** dynamic question generation, field validation, and progression indicators.  
- **Solution Mapper:** creation of solution paths with feasibility checks.  
- **FitScore Engine:** scoring logic, threshold-based lead categorization, and recalculation rules.  
- **CRM Intelligence Sync:** secure, duplicate-free lead synchronization with audit logging.  
- **Sales Copilot:** integrated view of lead status, missing information, and next-step guidance.

structure.title: Acceptance Criteria
structure.summary: The partial document outlines functional and acceptance criteria for a CRM-integrated lead management and discovery system. It covers requirements for different user roles and system components, including CRM Admin, External Prospect, and modules such as Knowledge Core, Discovery Engine, Solution Mapper, FitScore Engine, CRM Intelligence Sync, Sales Copilot, Discovery Chatbot, Founder Review, and Learning Layer. The main points include structured field mapping, duplicate lead prevention, intelligent and non-repetitive chatbot interactions, service and discovery management, solution recommendation logic, scoring and lead band calculation, CRM synchronization, sales user interaction with lead data, conversation context management, escalation and review processes, and continuous learning from overrides and outcomes. Additionally, it defines screen-wise requirements for the Lead Intelligence Dashboard and Live Discovery Assistant, specifying what data should be displayed and user actions available.

structure.title: Screen-wise Requirements
structure.summary:
The partial document outlines the functional and interface requirements for a lead management and discovery system. It includes user interactions, chatbot capabilities, decision review processes, learning mechanisms, detailed screen specifications, and workflow definitions. Key points include:  

- **Override & Review Processes:** Sales users and founders can override system recommendations, with reasons tracked and used for system learning.  
- **Discovery Chatbot:** The chatbot maintains context across multi-turn conversations, avoids repeating questions, provides lead summaries, and escalates to humans for complex or low-confidence cases.  
- **Founder Review:** Displays consolidated lead details—score, confidence, risks, and recommendations—allowing founders to approve, edit, or override decisions.  
- **Learning Layer:** Stores lead outcomes and overrides, monitors quality and accuracy trends, and controls logic update approvals before deployment.  
- **Screen-Wise Requirements:** Defines data displays and user actions for five main interfaces—Lead Intelligence Dashboard, Live Discovery Assistant, Solution Recommendation Panel, Founder Review Panel, and Knowledge Admin Panel—each specifying key fields and permissible actions.  
- **Workflow and Status Definitions:** Establishes standard lead and proposal readiness states governing progression from new lead to closure.  

Overall, the document specifies system functionality supporting intelligent lead handling, human oversight, iterative learning, and structured user interface design.

structure.title: Workflow State Definitions
structure.summary: The partial document outlines administrative, workflow, integration, and release planning specifications for a knowledge management or CRM system. It includes functions for approval, editing, ownership changes, and feedback submission within the Knowledge Admin Panel, which must display lists of services, industries, question banks, version history, and status indicators, along with options to add, edit, archive, change question conditions, upload case studies, and publish new versions. It defines workflow state categories including lead, proposal readiness, and confidence states to standardize process stages. Integration specifications cover CRM data synchronization, communication integrations via chat, WhatsApp, email, and future voice capabilities, as well as retrieval inputs like case studies and proposal archives. The release plan organizes development across sprint themes focused first on finalizing data models and taxonomies, then on building the knowledge administration and record management layers.

structure.title: Integration Specification Summary
structure.summary: The partial document outlines sections of a scoped proposal focused on integration specifications and a release plan. It details integration requirements for CRM and communication systems, including lead management, data synchronization, chat and email integration, and plans for future voice transcript handling. The document also lists types of retrieval inputs like case studies, service documents, and proposal archives. Additionally, it presents a release plan organized by sprint themes: the first sprint focuses on finalizing data models, taxonomies, and frameworks, while the second emphasizes building knowledge administration capabilities and creating service and industry records.

structure.title: Release Plan by Sprint Theme
structure.summary: The partial document outlines specifications and planning details for a scoped proposal system integrating CRM and communication platforms. It covers confidence states and integration summaries including CRM functions such as lead creation, updates, field and owner synchronization, task creation, and activity logging, as well as communication integrations like chat, WhatsApp, email ingestion, and voice transcript handling. It defines retrieval input types—case studies, service documents, pitch decks, proposal archives, transcripts, and SOPs. The release plan is structured by sprint themes, detailing development phases from data model finalization to dashboard building and external bot pilots. The next deliverables include detailed CRM mapping, service taxonomy, discovery question matrices, scoring models, wireframes, API contracts, and pilot rollout plans. Finally, the document specifies CRM field design principles for the AIM Entity Brain, emphasizing structured, auditable commercial data and clear field sources, covering lead identity, company intelligence, and contact authority fields.

structure.title: Recommended Next Deliverables
structure.summary: The partial document outlines sprint planning themes and deliverables for developing an AI-driven sales and discovery platform. It details objectives across multiple sprints, including building core components such as a field-state model, discovery engine, next-best question selector, scoring engine, lead intelligence dashboard, sales copilot interface, and CRM synchronization features. Subsequent sprints focus on founder review workflows, evaluation dashboards, external discovery bots, and pilot testing initiatives. The section on recommended next deliverables specifies creation of supporting artifacts like CRM field mapping, service taxonomy, discovery question matrices, scoring models, interface wireframes, API contracts, and pilot rollout plans. The document concludes with guidelines for defining a detailed CRM field structure, emphasizing clean, structured data, clear source attribution for fields, distinction between confirmed and inferred information, support for both human and AI input, and ensuring CRM data enables downstream sales processes and auditability. The subsections identify key categories of CRM fields: lead identity, company intelligence, and contact authority.

structure.title: Detailed CRM Field Mapping Sheet
structure.summary:
The partial document outlines development themes and detailed specifications related to building the AIM Entity Brain system. Early sections list sprint themes focused on creating components such as a field-state model, discovery engine logic, next-best question selector, scoring engine, lead intelligence dashboard, sales copilot interface, CRM sync, founder workflow, and external discovery bot testing.  

Subsequent sections define next deliverables and move into a thorough description of CRM field mapping standards. The CRM mapping portion establishes design principles for structured, auditable, and commercially useful data, recommending specific fields and enumerated value sets for lead identity, company intelligence, contact authority, requirements, timelines, budgets, qualifications, scoring, routing, and solution recommendations. It specifies required field sets at different lead stages, rules for prioritizing data sources, CRM object structures (Lead, Account, Contact, Opportunity, Activity, and AI-generated Intelligence Snapshot), and the system events needed to support automation and auditability.

Implementation notes emphasize use of structured enumerations, AI traceability, version control, duplicate detection, and separate tracking of score components. The document then identifies the next artifacts to create—service taxonomy and discovery question matrix—followed by a service taxonomy section defining AIM’s core service categories and design principles to ensure correct inquiry classification, service fit assessment, and consistent discovery and proposal paths. Core categories include product sampling, brand activation, retail branding, mobile outreach, and various venue- or audience-specific activation types.

structure.title: Service Taxonomy Sheet
structure.summary: The partial document outlines CRM data structuring rules, AI data traceability methods, and the design of a service taxonomy and discovery framework for AIM’s “Entity Brain” system. It emphasizes maintaining detailed, traceable AI-generated fields, preserving version metadata, and ensuring that duplicate detection uses multiple identifiers. The document then transitions to defining the AIM Service Taxonomy, which classifies various marketing and brand activation services into structured categories for production use. These include Product Sampling, On-Ground Brand Activation, Retail/In-Store Branding, Transit/Mobile Outreach, and Hybrid Campaign Solutions, each with clear descriptions, primary use cases, mandatory inputs for quoting and feasibility, and identified risks. It also introduces principles for differentiating service categories from touchpoints, defining misfit logic to exclude unviable opportunities, and ensuring discovery paths for each service. Finally, it covers the Discovery Question Matrix, which defines structured, purpose-based questioning for lead qualification, quoting, and feasibility checks, including universal and service-specific discovery questions categorized by their criticality to scoring, feasibility, and proposal generation.

structure.title: Discovery Question Matrix by Service and Industry
structure.summary: The partial document outlines an operational framework for evaluating opportunities and conducting structured discovery within a lead management or sales automation system referred to as the “Entity Brain.” It describes criteria for determining industry fit and disqualifying unsuitable leads based on factors such as mismatched audiences, unrealistic budgets, infeasible timelines, or lack of permissions. It then presents a comprehensive “Discovery Question Matrix” that guides how the system should gather information across various service types and industries. The section defines principles for question design, question tagging by criticality (e.g., must-ask, quote-critical, feasibility-critical), and priority rules for sequencing questions. It lists multiple service-specific and industry-specific discovery modules, stop rules to mark when discovery is complete, and escalation rules for complex or high-value cases. The end of the excerpt introduces the next document in the workflow—the Scoring Configuration Sheet—which establishes a scoring model to prioritize high-quality leads, route them effectively, and maintain consistent qualification standards across the organization.

structure.title: Scoring Configuration Sheet
structure.summary:
The partial document outlines the service-specific discovery and scoring framework for AIM’s lead qualification and management system. It covers procedures and logic for assessing potential projects and prospects through structured questioning, scoring metrics, and routing rules. Key sections include:

- **Service-Specific Discovery Rules:** Questions and processes for different outreach campaign types (LED van, hybrid campaigns) and industry segments (FMCG, beauty, wellness, baby care, etc.).
- **Question and Stop Rules:** Prioritization criteria for discovery questions and definitions of conditions under which the discovery process should pause or escalate.
- **Escalation Conditions:** Guidelines for when to involve senior leadership, such as in high-value or ambiguous cases.
- **Scoring Configuration Sheet:** A comprehensive scoring model defining quantitative evaluation for fit, authority, urgency, budget, completeness, strategic value, feasibility, and confidence, each with specific scoring bands, adjustment rules, and rationale.
- **Routing and Readiness Logic:** Rules for routing leads based on total score and confidence; defines proposal readiness stages and conditions for escalating complex leads.
- **Override and Configurability Parameters:** Policies for manual score corrections, recording overrides, and defining admin-level configurable elements like thresholds and penalties.
- **UI and Wireframe Guidance:** Requirements for a Lead Intelligence Dashboard, emphasizing clarity of scores, missing data, next steps, and layout structure for efficient lead assessment and user interaction.

Overall, the document serves as a detailed operational and design specification to convert AIM’s logic into a functional, systematic lead intelligence and qualification platform.

structure.title: Wireframe Requirements
structure.summary:
The partial document outlines functional and structural requirements for a product called AIM, focusing on wireframes and API specifications. It defines the goals, sections, fields, actions, and acceptance criteria for several key user interface screens:

1. **Lead Intelligence Dashboard** – Provides a complete view of a sales lead, including identification, qualification, scores, confidence levels, and escalation indicators. Designed for quick understanding of lead quality and missing information.
2. **Live Discovery Assistant** – Enables structured data collection during lead discovery, preventing repeated questions, updating scores dynamically, and suggesting relevant next questions.
3. **Solution Recommendation Panel** – Presents recommended commercial solutions with explanations, alternatives, risks, and supporting case studies, ensuring transparency in recommendations.
4. **Founder Review Panel** – Allows senior leaders to review, correct, or override lead recommendations quickly, with all changes logged and system suggestions visible.
5. **Knowledge Admin Panel** – Lets administrators manage service logic, question banks, scoring configurations, and version history securely and transparently.

Cross-screen requirements include consistent visual elements (score, confidence, readiness chips, badges) and behaviors emphasizing data traceability, human overrides, and clarity of inferred versus confirmed information. It also defines the recommended prototype build order according to business value and mentions that the next document should detail API contracts, event schemas, and rollout plans.

The API Contract Specification section establishes design principles for AIM Entity Brain APIs, supporting functions such as lead intake, discovery progression, scoring, recommendations, CRM synchronization, overrides, and analytics, with focus on versioning, traceability, structured data, and explicit business object design.

structure.title: API Contract Specification
structure.summary: The partial document outlines the design standards and API specifications for the AIM Entity Brain system, focusing on lead intelligence, discovery automation, scoring, and recommendation processes. It defines required behaviors for data traceability, human overrides, and visual differentiation of data types. It recommends prototype development order and specifies the next document to follow for integration clarity. The document provides detailed API contracts for lead intake, classification, discovery session management, dynamic question generation, answer capture, discovery summary, scoring, recommendations, proposal readiness, CRM synchronization, founder overrides, outcome logging, and knowledge management. Each API includes endpoint definitions, purposes, request/response examples, and operational rules emphasizing auditability, confidence handling, model version tracking, and consistency. It also lists analytics APIs, error handling standards with structured error formats, and an event schema specification that defines event-driven workflows for auditability, retries, analytics, and async integration. The design principles throughout stress explicit versioning, traceability, role-based access control, human-AI collaboration, and event-driven automation for robust and transparent business intelligence operations.

structure.title: Event Schema Specification
structure.summary: The partial document outlines the AIM Entity Brain’s event-driven architecture and pilot rollout strategy. It begins with an example of an error response format, then defines an Event Schema Specification describing how the system uses events for auditability, analytics, and workflow automation. It specifies event design principles, including idempotency and required metadata, and provides a standard event envelope format. The document categorizes core events into multiple groups such as Lead Events, Discovery Events, Scoring and Recommendation Events, CRM and Workflow Events, Human Review Events, and Learning and Outcome Events, each with specific triggers and purposes. Example event payloads illustrate how data is structured for different event types. It also lists recommended event consumers (e.g., CRM integration and analytics workers) and retry/failure handling guidelines to ensure reliability. The next section, “Pilot Rollout Plan,” explains how to introduce the first working version of AIM Entity Brain, emphasizing phased deployment, internal testing, founder oversight, and measurement of performance and business value. It defines the initial pilot scope, focusing on service types central to commercial operations, and recommends initial roles and users for the pilot program.

structure.title: Pilot Rollout Plan
structure.summary:
The partial document outlines the pilot rollout plan and early engineering implementation strategy for AIM’s “Entity Brain” system. It begins with operational reliability guidelines for handling system failures across CRM syncs, external messaging, and asynchronous processes. The core of the document focuses on the pilot rollout plan, describing the approach to introducing the first working version in a controlled, measurable way. Key pilot principles emphasize starting with high-impact services, restricting automation scope initially, maintaining tight founder oversight, and focusing on measurable business value.

The pilot scope prioritizes sales-related services such as product sampling, brand activation, and transit outreach. It defines pilot user roles—sales head, sales users, founder, operations, and product/admin—and details their responsibilities during the trial. Recommended pilot channels include manual and semi-automated lead intake methods, deferring autonomous tools until later.

Performance evaluation is structured around operational, quality, and commercial success metrics, such as discovery completeness, proposal readiness time, lead prioritization, and score accuracy. The rollout is divided into clear stages—from preparation and shadow mode to assisted live mode, controlled operation, and final review—each with specific actions, timelines, and feedback mechanisms. Governance cadence is defined at daily, weekly, and end-of-pilot levels to ensure continuous review and adjustment.

The document also provides a weekly pilot review template for analyzing lead processing data, system accuracy, and process improvement opportunities. Expansion criteria are outlined, along with potential next-phase projects involving broader branding workflows, customer-facing discovery bots, and automated prospecting. It concludes by recommending related deliverables such as wireframes, engineering breakdowns, data migration plans, and operating procedures. The final section begins detailing the engineering task breakdown, emphasizing modular design, proper sequencing, and building core logic before advanced user interfaces.

structure.title: Engineering Task Breakdown by Module
structure.summary: The partial document outlines the transition from a pilot phase to full-scale implementation of the AIM system, detailing evaluation, expansion planning, engineering breakdown, and knowledge ingestion. It begins with guidelines for assessing pilot performance through metrics such as lead processing, scoring accuracy, and service gap identification, followed by criteria for expansion into subsequent phases and candidate areas for extension. It then defines the ownership model and next supporting documents needed to move from planning to build execution. The engineering section translates product specifications into 11 structured workstreams covering platform foundation, knowledge core, retrieval and orchestration engines, API development, CRM integration, multiple frontends (sales copilot, founder review, knowledge admin), analytics and QA, along with suggested sprint sequencing, team composition, and risk mitigation strategies. Finally, a knowledge ingestion plan specifies how to convert various structured, semi-structured, and unstructured founder and company assets into normalized, validated, and traceable entities for AIM’s operational knowledge base.

structure.title: Knowledge Ingestion Plan
structure.summary: The partial document outlines a comprehensive plan for knowledge ingestion and pilot operations for AIM’s Entity Brain. It details how existing organizational knowledge—ranging from structured data like service lists to unstructured founder insights—should be transformed into structured, validated, and usable intelligence. Key points include principles for ingestion, prioritized knowledge types, and a multi-stage ingestion pipeline covering inventory, prioritization, extraction, normalization, validation, and publication. Templates and frameworks are provided for structuring services, case studies, objections, feasibility data, and founder reasoning. The document emphasizes accuracy, commercial relevance, and operational grounding, supported by a freshness policy and 30-day rollout plan. It highlights roles, quality checks, and common pitfalls to avoid in the ingestion process. The transition section outlines the Pilot Operating SOP, establishing how sales, founders, and operations should interact with the system during the pilot phase, ensuring continuous improvement through real usage, overrides, and weekly logic reviews.

structure.title: Pilot Operating SOP
structure.summary: The partial document outlines procedures and best practices for launching and managing a pilot phase of a system called AIM, focusing on knowledge ingestion, validation, and operational governance. It begins with feasibility checks and common ingestion mistakes, then transitions into detailed Standard Operating Procedures (SOPs) for different pilot roles and workflows. Key sections define how AIM’s Entity Brain should be used during real sales discovery, founder review, and operations validation to ensure pilot performance reflects both software and process reliability. Each role—sales, founder, ops, sales head, product/admin—is assigned clear responsibilities and daily actions. SOPs cover lead intake, discovery, escalation criteria, feasibility checks, monitoring practices, error tracking, and issue management. The document also prescribes daily and weekly cadences for reviews, logic updates, and training refinements to improve system accuracy and usability. Additional frameworks describe how leads should be handled by priority, how overrides serve as training signals for system logic, and what information must be included before proposal handoff. Overall, the document establishes a structured operating model for piloting AIM with continuous feedback, validation, and logic improvement cycles.

nodes.title: CRM Hygiene SOP During Pilot
nodes.summary: The partial document outlines several Standard Operating Procedures (SOPs) related to a pilot phase for CRM and sales system implementation. It covers CRM hygiene requirements, including monitoring duplicates, field mismatches, missing mandatory data, invalid enumerations, and task failures, emphasizing auditability and proper data ownership between CRM and Entity Brain systems. It defines training guidelines for sales users, covering system purpose, data interpretation, usage of AI suggestions, escalation protocols, and proper data correction methods through structured training sessions. It provides a classification framework for pilot issues to ensure feedback is actionable by categorizing problems into areas like knowledge gaps, scoring errors, system sync, usability, routing, feasibility, training, and performance. It outlines criteria for reviewing pilot success based on reduced founder dependency, improved discovery quality, early lead identification, feasibility detection, proper adoption, and system stability, with decision options for expansion or improvement. Finally, it introduces a daily pilot SOP checklist for routine monitoring and execution.

nodes.title: Training SOP for Sales Users
nodes.summary: The partial document outlines several Standard Operating Procedures (SOPs) related to managing a CRM pilot. It covers CRM hygiene and monitoring responsibilities for admins, emphasizing data accuracy, synchronization integrity, and auditability. It specifies training protocols for sales users, highlighting system understanding, data handling, and best practices to ensure effective system use. The document defines how to classify pilot issues into clear, actionable categories to streamline troubleshooting. It also establishes a framework for pilot success reviews, listing key metrics and decision options for expansion or correction. Finally, it references a daily checklist for operational consistency during the pilot phase.

nodes.title: Pilot Issue Classification SOP
nodes.summary: The partial document outlines several Standard Operating Procedures (SOPs) related to running a pilot program for a CRM-integrated system. It includes CRM hygiene guidelines requiring regular monitoring of data integrity issues such as duplicate leads, field mismatches, and failed synchronization. It sets rules ensuring CRM remains the operational record while maintaining auditability between systems. The training SOP emphasizes preparing sales users on system functionality, proper field handling, and understanding system logic through structured sessions. The issue classification SOP standardizes pilot feedback into defined categories for precise troubleshooting. The success review SOP establishes criteria for assessing pilot effectiveness and options for next steps based on outcomes. Finally, it introduces a pilot checklist to guide daily operational consistency.

nodes.title: Pilot Success Review SOP
nodes.summary: The partial document outlines standard operating procedures (SOPs) for managing and evaluating a pilot phase involving CRM and sales system integration. It covers guidelines for CRM hygiene, specifying that administrators must monitor data accuracy and integrity, with clear auditability between systems. It describes the training requirements for sales users, detailing the key concepts they must understand and the recommended multi-session training format. The document defines how to classify pilot issues into specific categories to ensure actionable feedback. It also details success review criteria for leadership, focusing on performance indicators, adoption, and system stability, and lists possible expansion decisions based on outcomes. Finally, it introduces a daily checklist for pilot management tasks.

nodes.title: Pilot SOP Checklist
nodes.summary: The partial document outlines Standard Operating Procedures (SOPs) and design guidelines for the pilot phase of the AIM Entity Brain system. It covers CRM hygiene management to ensure data accuracy and auditability, training requirements for sales users to properly use and understand the system, and issue classification methods to standardize problem reporting during the pilot. It defines pilot success review criteria and decision options for expansion based on performance metrics and adoption. The document also provides daily, weekly, and end-of-pilot checklists to track progress and readiness for scaling. Following operational SOPs, it transitions to design documentation, introducing the Figma Wireframe/Prototype Prompt Pack. This section guides UI/UX development with objectives for creating design-ready screen concepts, emphasizing a clean, data-driven, professional SaaS interface and core UX principles such as speed, clarity, and visibility of key lead information.

nodes.title: Recommended Next Document
nodes.summary: The partial document outlines operational and design guidance for a pilot program and its transition to product design. It begins with a checklist covering weekly and end-of-pilot tasks such as managing pilot leads, reviewing escalations, analyzing metrics, evaluating overrides, and assessing readiness for expansion. It then recommends the next document—a Figma Wireframe/Prototype Prompt Pack—which is intended to convert product specifications into design-ready prompts. The section specifies the design objectives, target users (designers, prototypers, frontend teams), and desired UI characteristics for the AIM Entity Brain interface. It emphasizes a clean, data-driven B2B SaaS design with clear information hierarchy and quick decision-making support, along with global UX principles ensuring clarity, visibility of key data points, and intuitive user understanding within 30 seconds.

structure.title: Figma Wireframe / Prototype Prompt Pack
structure.summary: The partial document outlines operational and design specifications related to the AIM Entity Brain project. It begins with procedural checklists for pilot operations, covering areas like lead management, escalation handling, synchronization issues, and performance reviews. It then transitions into guidance for the next development step: creating a Figma Wireframe / Prototype Prompt Pack. This section defines the purpose of transforming the AIM Entity Brain product specification into actionable design prompts for teams such as designers, UI/UX specialists, and front-end developers. It specifies design goals emphasizing a premium, clean, and data-driven B2B SaaS interface with strong usability principles—clarity, speed, and minimal clutter. Detailed design directions include color themes, layout structure, and interface components aligned with AIM’s brand. The document further provides master and screen-level prompts, such as for the Lead Intelligence Dashboard and Live Discovery Assistant, describing required UI elements, layout organization, and usability priorities for sales workflows.

nodes.title: Global Design Prompt
nodes.summary: The partial document outlines detailed design guidelines for a premium B2B SaaS product called AIM Entity Brain, an AI-powered sales intelligence copilot. It defines overall design objectives—modern, clean, data-driven visual language with clear distinction between AI suggestions and confirmed human data. The document covers requirements for UI behavior during live sales calls, emphasizing minimal scrolling and clear access to critical actions like assign, escalate, or propose. It includes specific prompts for two screen designs: the Lead Intelligence Dashboard, detailing layout, information hierarchy, and elements such as score displays, cards for recommendations, missing data, and case studies; and the Live Discovery Assistant, intended for real-time interaction support. Across both, emphasis is placed on visualization clarity, modular components, quick actions, and premium enterprise aesthetics suitable for sales teams and CRM workflows.

nodes.title: Screen Prompt: Lead Intelligence Dashboard
nodes.summary: The partial document outlines UI and design specifications for a premium B2B SaaS product called AIM Entity Brain, an AI-powered sales intelligence and discovery platform. It emphasizes usability during live calls, ensuring minimal scrolling and clear visibility of important actions such as asking the next question, assigning, escalating, or syncing. The design must visually distinguish AI-generated recommendations from human-confirmed information. It details a global design approach featuring a modern, data-driven, premium interface with modular cards, clear hierarchies, and defined zones for confirmed, inferred, missing, and recommended data. The document further provides screen-specific prompts for two modules: the Lead Intelligence Dashboard and the Live Discovery Assistant. The dashboard layout includes headers, status chips, cards for key information like company details, missing information, recommendations, proofs, and a sticky sidebar for scores and actions. The Live Discovery Assistant screen focuses on supporting real-time interactions during calls or messaging channels, maintaining clarity, efficiency, and visual separation between human and AI insights.

nodes.title: Screen Prompt: Live Discovery Assistant
nodes.summary: The partial document outlines detailed design prompts for different user interface components of a premium B2B SaaS product named “AIM Entity Brain,” an AI-powered sales intelligence and discovery assistant. It emphasizes a modern, clean, and data-driven design with a clear hierarchy and well-defined visual distinction between AI-generated and human-verified data. The main sections include: (1) a global design prompt defining the overall visual and UX style; (2) a “Lead Intelligence Dashboard” for viewing, scoring, and managing lead information through modular cards and quick actions; (3) a “Live Discovery Assistant” for real-time call or chat interactions enabling dynamic question flow, field tracking, and live score updates; and (4) a “Founder Review Panel” for executive review of high-value or low-confidence leads featuring override controls, audit trails, and system vs. human comparison views. Each section specifies key UI elements, layout structures, and usability principles to ensure fast, intuitive workflow support for sales teams.

nodes.title: Screen Prompt: Founder Review Panel
nodes.summary:
The partial document outlines detailed interface design specifications for multiple screens within the AIM Entity Brain system. It focuses on creating user experiences that are fast, clear, and purpose-driven for different roles such as salespersons, founders, and administrators. Key points covered include:

- **Sales Interaction Interface:** Describes a screen layout that supports real-time conversation with elements like a live transcript, dynamic “Next Best Question” card, structured answer capture, score and confidence updates, proposal readiness tracking, and quick actions for efficient sales flow.
- **Founder Review Panel:** Defines a review interface for high-value or uncertain leads, including score summaries, system-generated insights, override controls, comparison views, and audit timelines, emphasizing a premium, executive look with clear system-versus-human distinction.
- **Solution Recommendation Panel:** Outlines a screen to present strategic service recommendations, alternatives, rationale, assumptions, risks, and budget guidance with clear action routes to proposal preparation.
- **Knowledge Admin Panel:** Specifies an admin console for managing the system’s knowledge base—services, playbooks, questions, scoring, and versions—featuring tables, filters, status chips, and version control in a clean SaaS-style interface.
- **Weekly Pilot Analytics Dashboard:** Details an analytics dashboard used for pilot performance tracking, displaying KPIs, distribution charts, issue panels, and actionable weekly recommendations for continuous improvement.

nodes.title: Screen Prompt: Solution Recommendation Panel
nodes.summary:
The partial document outlines design specifications for three screens within the AIM Entity Brain system.  

1. **Solution Recommendation Panel** – A sales-focused interface guiding what service mix and campaign model to propose and why. It includes cards for primary and alternative recommendations, reasons for suitability, budget and timeline guidance, assumptions, exclusions or risks, and supporting case studies. The design emphasizes a strategic, premium look with clear hierarchy and commercial intelligence.  

2. **Knowledge Admin Panel** – An administrative console for managing internal knowledge assets such as services, industries, questions, scoring configurations, case studies, and logic versions. It features navigation menus, data tables or lists, editing drawers, version control, filters, and status indicators. The layout is intended to be clean, organized, and efficient, resembling a modern SaaS interface.  

3. **Weekly Pilot Analytics Dashboard** – A desktop dashboard used for pilot performance review by key stakeholders. It presents KPIs, analytical charts on scores and distributions, panels highlighting weak areas and issues, conversion metrics, and a weekly action recommendation section. The focus is on assessing pilot quality and operational insights through structured visual data.

nodes.title: Screen Prompt: Knowledge Admin Panel
nodes.summary:
The partial document outlines design prompts for three user interface screens within the AIM Entity Brain system.  
1. **Solution Recommendation Panel** – A screen aimed at helping sales teams determine suitable service proposals, including primary and secondary recommendation cards, business reasoning, budget and timeline guidance, assumptions and risk indicators, case study links, and key action buttons. The design should be strategic and commercially refined with clear hierarchy and premium styling.  
2. **Knowledge Admin Panel** – An internal admin console for managing services, industry playbooks, discovery questions, scoring configurations, case studies, and logic versions. It specifies navigation elements, data tables, version control features, status indicators, filters, and efficient, modern SaaS-style layout.  
3. **Weekly Pilot Analytics Dashboard** – A dashboard for weekly pilot performance review by leadership teams. It includes KPI cards, various analytical charts, panels for data quality issues, conversion analyses, and actionable weekly recommendations, emphasizing clarity and decision support.

nodes.title: Screen Prompt: Weekly Pilot Analytics Dashboard
nodes.summary: The partial document outlines design and UX prompts for various screens and components of a B2B SaaS product called AIM Entity Brain. It covers detailed specifications for multiple interfaces including the Solution Recommendation Panel, Knowledge Admin Panel, and Weekly Pilot Analytics Dashboard, each describing their purpose, content structure, and desired visual style. It also includes guidance for low-fidelity wireframes emphasizing layout and usability, a clickable prototype flow demonstrating core user interactions across key screens, and a component library defining reusable UI elements and design standards. The document concludes with UX copy guidance focused on concise, professional labeling and assistant-style system text for clarity and ease of use.

nodes.title: Low-Fidelity Wireframe Prompt Pack
nodes.summary: The partial document provides structured design and UX prompts for developing a B2B SaaS product called AIM Entity Brain. It outlines visual guidelines for a premium analytics interface with clean charts, a minimalist color scheme, and readable layouts. It includes detailed instructions for creating low-fidelity grayscale wireframes focusing on layout and usability for specific screens such as Lead Dashboard, Live Discovery Assistant, and Founder Review. It describes a clickable prototype flow that demonstrates user interactions across different modules, emphasizing workflow clarity. Additionally, it defines a component library with consistent design elements like cards, chips, badges, panels, and modals. The document concludes with UX copy guidance, recommending concise, professional labels and assistant-style system text to maintain clarity and usability throughout the prototype.

nodes.title: Clickable Prototype Flow Prompt

Overall, the document codifies AIM’s systematic process for designing, evaluating, and operationalizing product sampling services with explicit fit logic, structured data models, feasibility criteria, and procedural validation steps.

The document specifies output formats, including primary, secondary, and optional recommendations, plus a JSON structure for system outputs. It provides an industry-to-service ranking matrix covering multiple sectors (Beauty, FMCG, Wellness, Baby Care, Menstrual Hygiene, Pet Care), defining biases and service suitability. Meta rules link objectives and audience profiles to appropriate service types and establish environment, trust, and scale-vs-precision sensitivity guidelines. Tie-breaker criteria, pilot-first and hybrid campaign rules, and suppression conditions refine selection logic. A ranked matrix by objective pattern guides trial, education, sell-out support, and awareness strategies, followed by an algorithmic flow for the AIM recommendation engine. The closing sections include validation and publication checklists and propose a next deliverable—a decision tree and scenario test library—to validate matrix assumptions. The appended Beauty/Skincare playbook fragment introduces a focused guide for that industry’s commercial reasoning and service recommendation approach within AIM’s knowledge system.

structure.title: AIM Beauty / Skincare — Industry Playbook
structure.summary: The partial document provides a detailed framework for how AIM’s “Entity Brain” should process and recommend marketing or activation strategies within the Beauty/Skincare industry. It outlines validation checklists, publication readiness requirements, and subsequent related artifacts needed for implementation. The Beauty/Skincare Industry Playbook defines the industry scope, buyer personas, business objectives, target audience logic, and service-fit reasoning, emphasizing the importance of premium audience alignment, perception-sensitive environments, and guided product education. It details scoring cues, risk flags, feasibility factors, and response principles for objections. The document also specifies JSON schema for structured data representation and case study tagging guidelines. Finally, it includes a plan for validating and finalizing the playbook, before moving on to the next industry playbook—FMCG/Snacks/Beverages—and introduces the AIM Industry-to-Service Decision Tree framework for scenario-based validation and testing of the system’s recommendation logic.

structure.title: AIM Industry-to-Service Decision Tree + Scenario Test Library
structure.summary: The partial document outlines AIM’s structured framework for validating, testing, and operationalizing its industry-to-service decision logic. It defines validation and publication readiness checklists for the Beauty/Skincare playbook, specifying necessary founder, sales, and operational reviews before release. The main section, “AIM Industry-to-Service Decision Tree + Scenario Test Library,” explains the purpose, design principles, and decision flow for an AI system that recommends marketing and product trial environments based on variables like industry, objective, audience, trust sensitivity, scale, and feasibility. It includes detailed decision trees for choosing between sampling, activation, visibility, and retail-focused strategies, supported by scenario-based test cases across industries (Beauty/Skincare, FMCG, Wellness, Baby Care, Menstrual Hygiene, Pet Care). Each scenario defines classified inputs, expected outputs, and reasoning to evaluate system performance, along with negative test cases to ensure safe failure behavior. The document prescribes evaluation rubrics, common failure patterns to avoid, testing cadences for different teams, and a JSON schema for scenario recording. Finally, it provides publication readiness tasks and recommends the next artifact, “Founder Calibration Workbook + Historical Lead Test Set,” to further refine rule accuracy. It concludes with a filled service-record section describing “Product Sampling (Core)”—defining its scope, inclusions, and exclusions as a foundational operational service record within AIM’s knowledge system.

structure.title: AIM Product Sampling (Core) — Filled Service-Record Pack
structure.summary: The partial document outlines AIM’s structured framework for creating and validating service records related to Product Sampling as part of its Entity Brain knowledge-ingestion system. It begins with recommendations for follow-up artifacts to refine rules and calibrate decision logic. The main portion defines the “Product Sampling (Core)” service, including its objectives, inclusions, exclusions, fit logic, audience segmentation, discovery question bank, quote and feasibility requirements, pricing drivers, objection handling principles, scoring cues, recommendation logic, risks, and case-study tagging guidelines. It establishes parent-child hierarchy for related services (e.g., IT Park, RWA, Mall, Retail, College, Gym sampling) and provides JSON schema for system ingestion. The document further includes validation and publication readiness checklists to guide review and sign-off, emphasizing founder and operational validation before release. The final section recommends IT Park Sampling as the next priority record to be developed for testing inheritance and calibration of parent-child logic in the AIM knowledge framework.

structure.title: AIM IT Park Sampling — Filled Service-Record Pack
structure.summary:
The partial document outlines structured records from AIM’s “Entity Brain” framework, focusing on the parent service “Product Sampling” and its child service “IT Park Sampling.” It details operational logic, validation requirements, risk management, and system intelligence design. Key areas covered include:

- Sampling service variants under “Product Sampling,” such as mall, retail, college, gym, and hybrid campaigns, along with associated risk flags like budget mismatch, unclear audience, and unrealistic timelines.  
- Publication readiness and validation checklists describing review steps before going live, including founder, sales, and operations review alongside case study mapping and system inheritance confirmation.  
- The “IT Park Sampling” section defines the service as office- or tech-park–based product sampling targeting working professionals and premium urban audiences.  
- Comprehensive breakdown of service scope—what is included and excluded, fit/misfit logic, audience definition, best-fit objectives, and industry-category alignment.  
- Execution models including promoter-led, kiosk/table-led, and engagement-linked sampling, each with optimal use cases and rules.  
- Discovery questions, quote and feasibility inputs, pricing drivers, and objection-handling guidance to standardize sales and operational decision-making.  
- Scoring cues, recommendation logic, and structured risk warnings for effective qualification and automation by the Entity Brain.  
- Guidance for tagging case studies, expected JSON structure for system ingestion, and validation steps before publishing the child-service record.  
- Recommendation for the next record to develop (“RWA / Apartment Sampling”) to complement the IT Park model and enhance parent-child recommendation logic.  
- Foundational context of the AIM Entity Brain PRD, highlighting its purpose to codify founder-level commercial knowledge into a scalable system that improves lead qualification, discovery quality, CRM data consistency, and sales team autonomy.

structure.title: AIM Entity Brain — Product Requirements Document (PRD)
structure.summary:
The partial document is a detailed Product Requirements Document (PRD) and associated functional specifications for the “AIM Entity Brain,” an internal commercial intelligence and discovery system designed to capture and scale founder-level commercial reasoning across sales operations. It outlines a comprehensive framework for automating lead qualification, discovery questioning, solution recommendation, and CRM synchronization, enabling sales teams to perform nuanced discovery with minimal founder intervention.

The document covers numerous structured sections, including problem statements, product vision, objectives, user roles, user problems, and scope definitions. It explains the system’s core principles—context-aware questioning, structured data handling, human oversight, and continuous learning. It decomposes the product into functional modules such as the Service Intelligence Engine, Industry Intelligence Engine, Discovery Engine, Solution Mapper, FitScore Engine, CRM Intelligence Sync, Sales Copilot, Discovery Chatbot, and Founder Review workflows. Each module’s purpose, inputs, outputs, and business rules are explicitly defined.
