The document’s main sections detail the problem of knowledge centralization among founders, the aim to create a scalable system that improves lead qualification, discovery, and CRM accuracy, and the product vision of an intelligent reasoning layer that understands offerings, asks context-appropriate questions, maps solutions, scores leads, routes them correctly, and learns from performance feedback.

nodes.title: Product Vision
nodes.summary: The partial document outlines the design and purpose of the AIM Entity Brain Product Requirements Document (PRD). It covers preparatory steps such as sales and operations validation, case study mapping, and sampling record recommendations. The core focus is on building an intelligent system that captures and structures founder-level commercial knowledge to enhance lead qualification and discovery. Key sections describe the product’s problem statement—highlighting dependency on founders, inconsistent CRM data, and variable discovery quality—and the vision to create a decision engine capable of reasoning, questioning, mapping, scoring, and learning. It defines primary and secondary product objectives aimed at improving sales efficiency, data quality, proposal readiness, and institutional memory. The document also identifies user groups including sales teams, founders, operations, and CRM admins, and lists the specific pain points the system aims to address, particularly around lead qualification, information sufficiency, and proposal creation.

nodes.title: Product Objectives
nodes.summary: The partial document outlines a sales process optimization initiative aimed at improving lead management and intelligence. The main points include primary objectives such as enhancing discovery quality, reducing founder dependency, speeding up the inquiry-to-proposal cycle, improving CRM data quality, and building a reusable intelligence layer for AI-driven sales tools. Secondary objectives focus on refining lead routing, minimizing effort on low-fit leads, standardizing requirement gathering, and ensuring consistency in proposals while strengthening institutional knowledge. It identifies key user groups including sales executives, senior sales staff, business development, operations, proposal, and CRM teams, along with planned external users like website visitors and inquiry prospects. The user problems highlight challenges faced by the sales team, such as uncertainty in questioning, quoting, solution pitching, and reliance on founders for complex inquiries.

nodes.title: Users
nodes.summary: The partial document outlines objectives and user-oriented aspects of a sales process improvement initiative. The primary objectives focus on enhancing lead discovery, reducing founder reliance in qualification and solutioning, speeding up the proposal process, improving CRM data quality, and building a reusable intelligence layer for AI-driven tools. Secondary objectives emphasize refining lead routing, minimizing effort on unsuitable leads, standardizing requirement gathering, ensuring proposal consistency, and developing long-term institutional memory. It identifies both primary and secondary user groups—mainly sales, operations, and support teams—along with external users in later phases. The document also lists key challenges faced by the sales team, such as uncertainty in inquiry handling, insufficient information for quoting, difficulty in determining service combinations, and overdependence on founders for nuanced inquiries.

nodes.title: User Problems to Solve
nodes.summary: The partial document outlines the key objectives, user roles, challenges, project scope, and guiding principles for a sales intelligence and automation initiative. The primary goals focus on improving lead discovery quality, reducing founder dependency, increasing proposal readiness speed, enhancing CRM data structure, and creating a reusable intelligence layer for sales and AI tools. Secondary objectives aim to refine lead routing, minimize low-fit effort, and standardize early-stage processes. It identifies primary and secondary user groups, including sales, operations, and product teams, as well as future external users. The section on user problems highlights inefficiencies in discovery, dependency on founders, incomplete data, overpromising, and unstructured CRM records. The defined scope covers components like service and industry intelligence, discovery and scoring engines, chatbots, and proposal readiness, while excluding fully autonomous pricing, contracts, or telecalling in early phases. The core principles emphasize asking only commercially relevant questions and avoiding repetition of known information.

nodes.title: Scope Definition
nodes.summary: The partial document outlines key organizational challenges, project scope, and product principles. It identifies recurring issues across three areas: founder-level concerns about sales inefficiencies and inconsistent commercial thinking, operational problems like overpromising and vague requirements, and CRM problems involving incomplete or poorly structured data. The scope section defines what's included in Phases 1–3, focusing on building intelligence and automation tools such as discovery and scoring engines, chatbots, and assessment systems, while excluding fully autonomous proposal, discounting, telecalling, or contract processes. The core product principles emphasize gathering only essential information that impacts commercial decisions and avoiding redundant inquiries.

nodes.title: Core Product Principles
nodes.summary:
The partial document outlines the definition, scope, and design approach for a commercial intelligence and sales automation system. It begins by summarizing major operational challenges across founders, operations, and CRM processes, highlighting recurring issues like incomplete briefs, non-standardized commercial thinking, vague requirements, and inconsistent data handling. It then defines what is in scope for the phased development—such as service and industry intelligence, a discovery engine, lead scoring, CRM field mapping, and human-in-the-loop features—and what remains out of scope, including fully autonomous pricing, discounting, and contract generation. The document further establishes core product principles focused on relevance, information efficiency, factual integrity, human oversight, and continuous learning from interactions. Lastly, it describes multiple product modules in detail:  
- **Service Intelligence Engine**, which codifies service structure, fit conditions, pricing, and deliverables.  
- **Industry Intelligence Engine**, which captures commercial behavior, buyer personas, objections, and contextual nuances across industries.  
- **Discovery Engine**, which manages structured questioning logic to fill information gaps, infer data, and guide CRM updates.  
- **Solution Mapper**, which converts gathered discovery data into actionable commercial recommendations.  
Overall, the document defines how the system will improve sales discovery, decision intelligence, and CRM consistency while maintaining human control and data-driven learning.

nodes.title: Product Modules
nodes.summary:
The partial document outlines a structured AI-driven commercial service intelligence system called AIM, detailing its operational principles, product modules, and CRM data model. It emphasizes clear separation of confirmed versus inferred facts, controlled decision-making, and continuous learning from interactions. The main modules include: 

- **Service Intelligence Engine:** Maintains structured data about services, sub-services, fit conditions, industries, and pricing to determine optimal service recommendations.
- **Industry Intelligence Engine:** Understands industry-specific commercial behaviors, goals, personas, and pitch strategies.
- **Discovery Engine:** Manages dynamic questioning to gather structured information efficiently and identify missing details or next-best questions.
- **Solution Mapper:** Translates discovery data into suitable commercial solutions, budget estimates, and rollout plans.
- **FitScore Engine:** Quantifies lead quality through multiple scoring parameters like fit, budget, authority, and urgency.
- **Proposal Readiness Engine:** Determines readiness for proposals and provides summaries, missing inputs, and suggested next actions.
- **CRM Intelligence Sync:** Converts conversation data into structured CRM updates, triggers tasks, and handles lead routing and escalation.
- **Sales Copilot:** A live interaction interface for salespeople to manage discovery, scoring, and recommendations efficiently.
- **Discovery Chatbot:** Automates initial inquiry handling through structured questioning and generates clean CRM leads.
- **Voice Prospecting Engine:** A future module designed for voice-based outreach and qualification with strong compliance controls.
- **Core Data Model:** Defines key objects and rules for consistent data structuring, including status types for confirmed, inferred, missing, or not applicable fields.
- **CRM Field Definitions:** Begins listing identity and classification fields for managing lead information within the system.

nodes.title: Core Data Model
nodes.summary: The partial document outlines governance and data structure guidelines for a customer relationship management (CRM) or sales enablement system. It emphasizes strong operational controls, including human escalation procedures, restriction on unsupported claims, and maintenance of an audit trail. The core data model section defines key system components such as Service and Sub-Service Masters, Industry Playbooks, Discovery Question Banks, Solution Rules, and libraries for case studies, objections, and proposals. It introduces the concept of field status types (confirmed, inferred, missing, not applicable) to ensure data accuracy. The CRM field definitions section specifies required attributes for lead management, including company and contact details, source information, and classification fields.

nodes.title: CRM Field Definitions
nodes.summary:
The partial document outlines specifications and operational guidelines for a CRM or lead management system focused on structured discovery, data governance, and intelligent routing. It emphasizes strong control measures, including human escalation protocols, validation of claims, and audit tracking. The core data model defines key entities such as Service Master, Industry Playbook, Lead Profile, and supporting libraries for case studies and objections. Each key field requires a status designation to distinguish confirmed data from assumptions.

Detailed CRM field definitions are provided across categories—identity, classification, requirements, intelligence, routing, and output—covering specifics like lead scoring, proposal readiness, and system-driven recommendations. The document also describes primary user flows: handling inbound leads via automated channels, salesperson-assisted discovery using guided logic, and founder-level escalation for high-value or low-confidence cases. These flows demonstrate how the system integrates automation, human input, and feedback loops. Finally, it introduces interface expectations, beginning with a Lead Intelligence Dashboard to visualize and manage insights and actions.

nodes.title: Key User Flows
nodes.summary:
The partial document outlines the discovery and sales process automation for handling inbound leads and assisted discovery within a CRM or sales platform. It focuses on how the system captures and classifies leads, guides interactions through intelligent questioning, scores leads in real time, and supports salespersons and founders in decision-making. The main sections describe:  
- Automated discovery workflows for inbound leads from the website or WhatsApp, including classification, scoring, and proposal readiness evaluation.  
- The salesperson-assisted discovery process, supported by a copilot that surfaces known data, suggests questions, and updates recommendations dynamically.  
- Founder review escalation for high-value or uncertain leads, where the system provides summarized insights and recommendations for final review or override.  
It also begins to specify interface and dashboard requirements, such as the design of a Lead Intelligence Dashboard to support these processes.

nodes.title: Screens / Interface Requirements
nodes.summary: The partial document outlines the design and functional specifications for an intelligent lead discovery and sales support system. It details key user flows for handling inbound leads, salesperson-assisted discoveries, and high-value founder review escalations. Each flow describes how data is collected, analyzed, scored, and escalated to ensure accurate proposal readiness and efficient lead management. The document specifies interface requirements for various panels, including dashboards for lead intelligence, discovery tracking, solution recommendations, founder reviews, and administrative knowledge management. Functional requirements are categorized into must-have, should-have, and future features, emphasizing capabilities like structured knowledge storage, automated questioning, scoring, proposal readiness detection, human oversight, and system learning through overrides. Additional planned enhancements include AI-driven communication, meeting scheduling, and automated proposal generation.

nodes.title: Functional Requirements
nodes.summary: The partial document outlines specifications for a sales or lead management system composed of multiple interactive panels and functional modules. It details the required components for various panels—such as Discovery, Solution Recommendation, Founder Review, and Admin Knowledge Management—each defining specific display or action requirements like live transcripts, scoring, next-best question suggestions, and case study management. The functional requirements emphasize capabilities including structured knowledge storage, context-aware questioning, dynamic lead scoring, CRM integration, and proposal readiness detection, with additional desirable and future features like automated proposals and predictive analytics. Non-functional requirements stress performance, security, scalability, auditability, and reliability for real-time use. Guardrails define commercial, reasoning, and operational constraints to ensure accuracy, compliance, and accountability. Finally, analytics and success metrics specify key performance indicators for sales productivity and lead quality, focusing on efficiency, discovery completeness, and qualification rates.

nodes.title: Non-Functional Requirements
nodes.summary: The partial document outlines system requirements and governance measures for a predictive win probability solution used in a sales context. It covers non-functional requirements such as performance for live use, stateful conversation handling, secure access control, auditability, scalability, CRM integration reliability, and fallback mechanisms for low-confidence predictions. It defines guardrails and governance categories—commercial (avoiding unauthorized commitments or discounts), reasoning (ensuring data accuracy and confidence management), and operational (logging overrides and reviewing high-value low-confidence cases). Finally, it specifies analytics and success metrics focusing on sales productivity (e.g., discovery completeness, time to proposal) and lead quality (e.g., qualified lead rate).

nodes.title: Guardrails and Governance
nodes.summary: The partial document outlines system specifications for a predictive win probability solution. It covers non-functional requirements such as performance for live use, multi-turn conversational support, secure and auditable interactions, version control, scalability, reliability, and fallback behaviors. It then defines guardrails and governance across commercial, reasoning, and operational dimensions, specifying approved logic for pricing, data handling standards, escalation procedures, and versioning practices. Finally, it lists analytics and success metrics focused on sales productivity and lead quality assessment, including measures like discovery completeness, proposal readiness time, and qualified lead rates.

nodes.title: Analytics and Success Metrics
nodes.summary: The partial document outlines design specifications and implementation plans for a predictive win probability sales assistant. It covers non-functional requirements such as speed for live use, secure and auditable operation, scalability, version control, and reliability in CRM synchronization. Guardrails and governance define strict commercial, reasoning, and operational boundaries to ensure compliance, accuracy, and accountability in system behavior. Analytics and success metrics describe how performance will be measured across sales productivity, lead quality, bot operation, and learning accuracy. The phased rollout plan details sequential implementation stages, beginning with establishing foundational knowledge and logic assets, followed by deployment as an internal sales copilot with dashboards, CRM integration, scoring, and proposal readiness tools.

nodes.title: Phased Rollout Plan
nodes.summary:
The partial document outlines metrics, a phased rollout plan, key risks, and an MVP definition for a sales or AI-driven lead management system. It lists performance and quality metrics such as lead conversion rates, disqualification accuracy, bot question relevance, and learning measures tied to overrides and recommendation accuracy. The phased rollout plan includes:  
- Phase 1 to build foundational knowledge structures like taxonomies, playbooks, and scoring models.  
- Phase 2 to launch an internal sales copilot with dashboards and discovery assistants.  
- Phase 3 to deploy an external discovery bot for automated lead qualification.  
- Phase 4 to introduce a voice prospecting engine with AI calling and objection handling.  
Identified risks include knowledge overload, weak logic, AI overpromising, low user adoption, and inaccurate scoring, each with specific mitigation strategies. The MVP focuses on core services, sub-services, and target industries.

nodes.title: Risks
nodes.summary: The partial document outlines phased goals and deliverables for improving lead discovery and prospecting through AI-driven tools. Phase 3 focuses on building an external discovery bot for consistent lead qualification via a website or WhatsApp interface, featuring stateful questioning, automatic lead structuring, and human handoff summaries. Phase 4 introduces a voice prospecting engine aimed at scaling outbound efforts with AI calling, transcript analysis, callback routing, objection capture, and a review dashboard. The document also lists major project risks and corresponding mitigations, such as managing document overload, ensuring effective discovery logic, preventing AI overpromising, encouraging user adoption, and refining scoring accuracy. Finally, the MVP definition specifies inclusion of core services, sub-services, and top target industries.

nodes.title: MVP Definition
nodes.summary: The partial document outlines a phased product development plan for AIM’s commercial intelligence and discovery system. It covers goals and deliverables for Phase 3 (External Discovery Bot) and Phase 4 (Voice Prospecting Engine), emphasizing automated lead qualification, AI-driven outreach, and scalable prospecting using a unified intelligence engine. It details risk management strategies addressing data overload, weak logic, AI reliability, user adoption, and early scoring accuracy. The MVP definition specifies included and excluded features, focusing on core services, lead discovery, scoring, and CRM integration. Launch recommendations prioritize a sequential rollout for optimal ROI and learning, starting internally before public exposure. Final recommendations advocate building AIM first as an internal copilot to strengthen discovery and CRM processes before expanding to customer-facing automation. It also lists next documents to develop, including functional specs, CRM mapping, ontology, user stories, and integration plans. The beginning of the module-wise functional specification section introduces the Knowledge Core, detailing its purpose and inputs for managing structured commercial intelligence.

nodes.title: Launch Recommendation
nodes.summary: The partial document outlines the design and launch strategy for AIM’s internal commercial intelligence and discovery copilot. It covers key components such as curated discovery questions, service recommendation logic, scoring engine, CRM writeback, and founder review capture. It specifies features excluded from the MVP phase, such as public chatbot access, voice calling, autonomous pricing, and proposal generation. The launch order prioritizes the Knowledge Core, Sales Copilot, CRM Intelligence Sync, External Discovery Bot, and Voice Prospecting Engine, emphasizing fast ROI and safe iterative learning. The final recommendation advises building AIM first for internal use to refine commercial intelligence, improve lead quality, and safely validate discovery logic before extending to customer-facing automation. The document also lists the next steps, including creating functional specifications, CRM mapping, knowledge models, user stories, API specs, and wireframes. The section on the Knowledge Core defines its purpose as storing and managing AIM’s structured commercial intelligence with inputs from service definitions, industry playbooks, and discovery questions.

nodes.title: Final Recommendation
nodes.summary: The partial document outlines the structure and launch strategy for an AI-based internal sales and discovery copilot system. It specifies key components such as curated discovery questions, service recommendation logic, scoring engine, CRM integration, and features for founder review and overrides. The document highlights which features are excluded from the MVP, such as public chatbot access, voice calling, autonomous pricing, and proposal generation. It provides a launch order emphasizing starting with internal tools like the Knowledge Core and Sales Copilot to ensure fast ROI and safe learning before expanding to public-facing features. Recommendations focus on building the system internally first to improve data quality, validate logic, and reduce dependency on founders. It also details the next set of required documents—functional specifications, CRM mappings, ontology, user stories, integration designs, and rollout plans. The section for the “Knowledge Core” module describes its purpose as storing structured commercial intelligence and lists key inputs, including service and sub-service definitions, industry playbooks, and discovery questions.

nodes.title: Next Document to Create
nodes.summary: The partial document outlines components and recommendations for developing an AI-driven commercial intelligence and discovery copilot. It includes functional and launch strategy details, such as curated discovery questions, a scoring engine, service recommendation logic, CRM integration, and internal review features. Certain elements are excluded from the MVP, specifically public chatbot access, voice calling, and autonomous pricing or proposal generation. It provides a phased launch plan prioritizing internal systems—Knowledge Core, Sales Copilot, CRM Intelligence Sync—before external-facing capabilities. The final recommendation emphasizes building the system as an internal copilot first to improve data quality, validate logic, and minimize risk. It also lists the next documents to create, including module specifications, CRM mappings, ontologies, user stories, API definitions, and wireframes. The section on the Knowledge Core module defines its purpose as managing structured commercial intelligence and outlines its key inputs.

nodes.title: Module-wise Functional Specification
nodes.summary:
The partial document outlines a detailed product requirements and functional design for a commercial intelligence and discovery system called AIM. It defines the core logic, exclusions, launch recommendations, and sequencing strategy to ensure fast ROI and controlled development. The system is built around several interconnected modules: Knowledge Core (for structured commercial intelligence management), Discovery Engine (for dynamic questioning and data gathering), Solution Mapper (for mapping discovered needs to solution paths), FitScore Engine (for lead scoring based on commercial logic), CRM Intelligence Sync (for structured CRM integration), Sales Copilot (to assist sales staff during live interactions), Discovery Chatbot (for external prospect qualification), Founder Review & Override (to allow human correction and oversight), and Learning & Evaluation Layer (to improve logic and performance through feedback). Each module includes purpose, inputs, core functions, outputs, business rules, dependencies, and admin actions.

The document additionally covers recommended launch order, emphasizing internal deployment before public chatbot release, and proposes subsequent documentation steps including functional specifications, CRM mapping, ontology models, user stories, and rollout plans. It concludes with detailed user stories for various roles—Sales Executive, Sales Head, Founder, Operations Manager, and CRM Admin—highlighting how each interacts with the system to improve discovery quality, lead prioritization, commercial consistency, and operational accuracy.

nodes.title: User Stories
nodes.summary: The partial document outlines system design principles, user stories, and acceptance criteria for a sales and lead management platform. It begins with references to evaluation dashboards, quality reports, and business rules emphasizing controlled logic updates and version-linked historical outcomes. The user stories section defines requirements for multiple roles—Sales Executive, Sales Head, Founder, Operations Manager, CRM Admin, and External Prospect—detailing desired functionalities like intelligent discovery questions, prioritization of high-intent leads, structured sales data capture, smooth handoffs, clean CRM records, and smart chatbot interactions. The acceptance criteria section describes technical and functional expectations for various system modules: Knowledge Core (service versioning and discovery field mapping), Discovery Engine (context-aware question selection and data confirmation), Solution Mapper (recommendation generation with risk flags), FitScore Engine (lead scoring logic), CRM Intelligence Sync (accurate data integration with logging), and Sales Copilot (comprehensive lead insights and override management). Overall, the document focuses on intelligent automation, data integrity, and user-centric workflow optimization across the sales process.

nodes.title: Acceptance Criteria
nodes.summary: The partial document outlines a set of functional and acceptance criteria for a CRM-based lead management and discovery system integrated with AI-driven components. It describes user stories for both CRM administrators and prospects, focusing on duplicate lead prevention and intelligent chatbot interactions. The acceptance criteria cover several system modules: Knowledge Core for managing service definitions and discovery fields; Discovery Engine for question selection and field management; Solution Mapper for generating recommendations and risk assessments; FitScore Engine for scoring leads; CRM Intelligence Sync for reliable lead creation and updates; Sales Copilot for assisting sales users with lead insights; Discovery Chatbot for maintaining conversational context and structured lead summaries; Founder Review for executive oversight of recommendations; and Learning Layer for storing outcomes and improving system logic. The document also outlines screen-level requirements for the Lead Intelligence Dashboard and Live Discovery Assistant, detailing necessary display elements and actions for user interaction with lead data, discovery processes, and CRM synchronization.

nodes.title: Screen-wise Requirements
nodes.summary: The partial document outlines functional requirements and workflow definitions for a lead management and discovery system incorporating a bot and human review. It describes how the bot maintains conversational context, avoids duplicate questions, produces structured summaries, and hands off to humans when necessary. It defines the founder review process, including system outputs like scores, confidence levels, and recommendations, with options for override and feedback to the learning layer. The learning layer section covers data storage, reporting on system performance, and controlled logic updates. Screen-wise requirements specify detailed display elements and actions for several interfaces: Lead Intelligence Dashboard, Live Discovery Assistant, Solution Recommendation Panel, Founder Review Panel, and Knowledge Admin Panel. Finally, workflow state definitions outline lead status, proposal readiness, and confidence states used throughout the system.

nodes.title: Workflow State Definitions
nodes.summary: The partial document outlines the structure and functionality of a Knowledge Admin Panel and related system components. It specifies required screen elements such as service and industry lists, question banks, version history, and user actions like adding, editing, archiving, changing conditions, uploading case studies, and publishing versions. It defines workflow states for lead management, proposal readiness, and confidence assessment. The integration summary describes CRM and communication integrations, including lead handling, field synchronization, activity logging, chat and messaging channels, and retrieval inputs like case studies and proposals. Finally, the release plan details sprint themes focused on data model finalization, taxonomy and framework setup, admin layer development, record building, field-state logic, and discovery engine logic implementation.

nodes.title: Integration Specification Summary
nodes.summary: The partial document outlines integration specifications and a release plan organized by sprint themes. It details CRM integration features such as lead management, field and owner synchronization, task creation, status updates, and activity logging. Communication integrations include website chat, WhatsApp, email ingestion, and planned voice or call transcript integration. It also lists types of retrieval inputs like case studies, service documents, proposals, and SOPs. The release plan is divided into three sprint themes covering activities such as finalizing data models and taxonomies, building administrative and service layers, developing field-state models, and implementing discovery engine logic.

nodes.title: Release Plan by Sprint Theme
nodes.summary: The partial document outlines integration specifications, release planning, and CRM field design for a system referred to as AIM Entity Brain. It summarizes integration types including CRM (lead creation and updates, field and owner sync, task creation, and activity logging) and communication channels (website chat, email, WhatsApp, and future voice/call transcripts). It lists retrieval input sources such as case studies, pitch decks, and SOPs. The release plan is organized by sprint themes—progressing from data model and taxonomy finalization to discovery engine development, dashboard and copilot interfaces, external bot beta, and pilot testing. Recommended next deliverables include documents like CRM field mapping, service taxonomy, question matrix, scoring configuration, wireframes, API schema, and rollout plan. The CRM field mapping section defines principles for structured, auditable data collection that supports AI and human input, downstream automation, and meaningful lead qualification, with subsections for lead identity, company intelligence, and contact authority fields.

nodes.title: Recommended Next Deliverables
nodes.summary: The partial document outlines functional specifications and planning details for a series of development sprints related to a sales and lead management system. It highlights upcoming sprint themes, focusing on building components such as a next-best question selector, scoring engine, lead intelligence dashboard, sales copilot interface, CRM synchronization, founder review workflow, logging, evaluation dashboards, and external discovery tools. Later sections present recommended next deliverables following the specification, including CRM field mapping, service taxonomy, discovery question matrices, scoring model configuration, wireframes, API contracts, and pilot rollout plans. The document also details CRM field design principles aimed at ensuring data quality, reliability, and actionable insights, with specific recommendations for lead identity, company intelligence, and contact authority fields, including defined decision-maker status values.

nodes.title: Detailed CRM Field Mapping Sheet
nodes.summary:
The partial document outlines successive development sprints for a project involving AI-driven sales and CRM intelligence tools, followed by detailed functional guidelines for CRM field design and data structuring. It covers tasks such as building question selectors, scoring engines, dashboards, and interfaces across multiple sprint themes. The major sections specify next deliverables and documents to be created—CRM field mapping, service taxonomy, discovery question matrices, scoring configuration, dashboard wireframes, and API schemas.

In the CRM Field Mapping section, it defines objectives to ensure structured, commercially useful, and auditable data from discovery interactions. It details principles for data source hierarchy, field design standards, and structured enums for routing, along with key field groups such as identity, company intelligence, contact authority, requirement classification, timeline, budget, qualification, scoring, routing, solution recommendations, channel details, audit fields, and minimum required fields by lead stage. It further specifies CRM object relationships (Lead, Account, Contact, Opportunity, Activity, Intelligence Snapshot) and event triggers supporting automation and auditability.

Implementation notes emphasize structured metadata, separate score components, duplication checks, and traceable AI updates. The document concludes with guidance for subsequent documents—Service Taxonomy and Discovery Question Matrix—followed by a section defining the initial Service Taxonomy framework for AIM Entity Brain. This taxonomy ensures correct classification of inquiries, proper service fit determination, and readiness for quoting and feasibility processes. It lists core Level 1 AIM service categories, including product sampling, brand activation, transit and retail outreach, as well as hybrid and environment-specific campaign solutions.

nodes.title: Service Taxonomy Sheet
nodes.summary: The partial document outlines guidelines and frameworks for structuring AIM’s CRM and service taxonomy system, focusing on how the “Entity Brain” should classify, process, and qualify service-related opportunities. It begins with instructions on data management and traceability, emphasizing duplicate detection, distinct readiness status fields, and data source tracking. The main body defines the Service Taxonomy Sheet, detailing the first production taxonomy design principles and Level 1 categories such as Product Sampling, On-Ground Brand Activation, Retail Branding, Transit Outreach, and Hybrid Campaigns. Each service category includes purpose, typical industries, deliverables, mandatory quote and feasibility inputs, and common operational risks. It also specifies how to handle fit and misfit logic to ensure service suitability and commercial viability. The section on the Discovery Question Matrix defines how the system determines what information to ask based on service and industry context, outlining principles for purposeful question design, mandatory versus optional queries, and their roles in qualification, quoting, feasibility, and scoring.

nodes.title: Discovery Question Matrix by Service and Industry
nodes.summary:
The partial document outlines operational logic and decision frameworks for a system called “Entity Brain,” focusing on lead qualification, discovery processes, and scoring mechanisms.  
It begins with rules for identifying and deprioritizing unsuitable opportunities based on mismatched audiences, unrealistic budgets or timelines, and feasibility issues.  
Next, it details a “Discovery Question Matrix” that defines structured questioning protocols by service type and industry, emphasizing how questions should map to decisions, affect scoring and feasibility, and be prioritized for efficiency. The matrix includes universal and service-specific question sets, industry add-ons, and rules to determine when discovery should stop or escalate to senior management.  
It concludes with the “Scoring Configuration Sheet,” which establishes principles for rating leads according to commercial quality, feasibility, completeness, and confidence, aiming to optimize prioritization, routing, and consistency in qualification across the organization.

nodes.title: Scoring Configuration Sheet
nodes.summary:
The partial document outlines structured rules, logic, and scoring methods for managing leads within a system called AIM Entity Brain. It begins with a service taxonomy listing categories like FMCG, Beauty, Wellness, Baby Care, and Pet Care, then establishes discovery and escalation protocols for handling client queries. The text defines question priority rules, stop criteria for moving leads to the next stage, and escalation conditions during discovery. It then transitions to a “Scoring Configuration Sheet,” detailing how AIM evaluates and prioritizes leads.

The scoring framework assigns a total of 100 points distributed across categories such as Fit, Authority, Urgency, Budget, Completeness, Strategic Value, and Feasibility, with additional confidence metrics. Each score type includes objectives, adjustment rules, and guidance ranges. The document also describes how confidence affects lead handling, lead readiness levels for proposal stages, and automated routing, escalation, and SLA recommendations based on score and confidence combinations.

Examples illustrate application of the model in different scenarios (strong qualified lead, vague high-value lead, and low-fit lead). Further sections specify override procedures, logging requirements, configuration flexibility for admins, and UI design recommendations that ensure transparency and enable real-time human corrections. Overall, the document establishes a comprehensive system to qualify, prioritize, and manage sales leads efficiently using consistent, rule-based scoring and escalation logic.

nodes.title: Wireframe Requirements
nodes.summary:
The partial document outlines wireframe and API requirements for a product called AIM Entity Brain. It defines the structure, goals, key actions, and acceptance criteria for several primary user interface screens across different roles. These screens include:

1. **Lead Intelligence Dashboard** – Provides sales and leadership an overview of a lead, summarizing scores, missing information, recommendations, and allowing quick actions like assigning owners or escalating leads.  
2. **Live Discovery Assistant** – Guides sales or bots through structured discovery of leads without repetition, showing conversation notes, field statuses, and live score updates.  
3. **Solution Recommendation Panel** – Displays recommended commercial solutions, supporting rationale, risks, assumptions, and links to case studies.  
4. **Founder Review Panel** – Enables leaders to review escalated leads, adjust recommendations, or override system suggestions in a traceable manner.  
5. **Knowledge Admin Panel** – Lets administrators manage underlying service logic, scoring, and question banks with version control and permissions.

The document also specifies cross-screen design requirements like traceability, override visibility, and highlighting missing critical data. It suggests a prototype development order prioritizing business value and outlines the next documentation milestone, focusing on API and integration plans.

Finally, it begins defining the **API Contract Specification**, describing API functions for lead management, scoring, recommendations, synchronizations with CRM, and audit capabilities. It emphasizes clear business object modeling, versioning, traceability of AI outputs, and support for human overrides.

nodes.title: API Contract Specification
nodes.summary: The partial document outlines technical and behavioral specifications for the AIM Entity Brain system, focusing on data traceability, human override visibility, and clear differentiation between confirmed, inferred, and missing information. It establishes prototype build priorities and recommends subsequent documentation for API and rollout planning. The majority of the content defines the API Contract Specification, detailing endpoints and rules for lead management processes—such as intake, classification, discovery sessions, question generation, answer capture, scoring, recommendation, proposal readiness, CRM synchronization, founder overrides, and outcome logging. It also specifies authentication models, access roles, and authorization rules for different system users. Additional sections describe Knowledge Management APIs for maintaining service, question, and industry data with version control; Analytics APIs for performance insights; standardized error handling formats; and an Event Schema Specification for event-driven integration, ensuring all lead-related changes are traceable, versioned, and auditable within an asynchronous workflow architecture.

nodes.title: Event Schema Specification
nodes.summary: The partial document defines the event schema specification for the AIM Entity Brain’s event-driven architecture. It describes the principles of event design emphasizing auditability, retries, analytics, and asynchronous workflow automation. It specifies that key lead actions and state changes must emit structured events containing metadata such as event ID, type, timestamp, source, and versions. The document categorizes events into multiple groups: core lead events (e.g., creation, updates, escalation), discovery events (related to sessions and captured answers), scoring and recommendation events (score calculation and confidence updates), CRM and workflow events (sync operations and task management), human review events (override and review approvals), and learning/outcome events (recording results and publishing knowledge versions). It provides example event payloads for score calculation and lead escalation, identifies recommended consumers such as CRM integration, analytics, notification, audit logging, and model evaluation services, and establishes retry and failure handling rules ensuring resilience and proper error management.

nodes.title: Pilot Rollout Plan
nodes.summary: The partial document outlines a detailed pilot rollout plan for launching the first working version of “Entity Brain” within AIM. It emphasizes proving business value quickly and safely rather than full automation from the start. Key principles include focusing on high-frequency, commercially significant services, starting with internal users, maintaining tight feedback loops, and tracking both accuracy and business impact before scaling. The plan specifies the pilot scope—covering product sampling, brand activation, and mobile outreach services—and identifies pilot users such as sales leads, founders, operations, and product owners with defined roles. It prescribes initial channels (sales copilot, website, WhatsApp) while deferring automation-heavy features. Success criteria are detailed across operational, quality, and commercial metrics, with sample KPIs for benchmarking. The rollout is structured into staged phases from preparation through controlled operation, culminating in a review and expansion decision. Lastly, governance cadence is defined with daily and weekly reviews, closing with a performance comparison and next-phase planning.

nodes.title: Pilot Governance Cadence
nodes.summary: The partial document outlines the phased rollout and governance plan for implementing a sales copilot system. It begins with the system running parallel to existing sales processes to compare its suggestions against actual actions and log discrepancies. Subsequent stages describe increasing levels of system involvement—from assisted live use by the sales team and operational validation to controlled deployment for selected leads with automated discovery summaries and scoring. The final stage focuses on reviewing performance metrics, override patterns, question quality, and routing accuracy to decide on broader expansion. Governance activities include daily and weekly reviews of sync issues, escalations, overrides, and quality metrics, culminating in a pilot-end assessment comparing baseline and pilot results and defining next-phase plans.

nodes.title: Recommended Pilot Review Template
nodes.summary: The partial document outlines recommendations and guidelines for reviewing, expanding, and implementing a pilot program, followed by steps to transition into engineering execution. It includes a pilot review template covering metrics such as lead processing, scoring, escalation, and classification issues. The expansion criteria specify when to proceed to later phases based on quality, reliability, and trust metrics, with suggested expansion opportunities for Phases 2 and 3. It lists subsequent documents needed to move from planning to build execution—such as prototypes, engineering task breakdowns, and migration plans. The engineering section begins the task breakdown by module, explaining its purpose in estimating effort, assigning responsibilities, and sequencing work. Key design principles emphasize building foundational data and logic before UI, isolating testable workstreams, and treating knowledge quality as a core product dependency.

nodes.title: Expansion Criteria After Pilot
nodes.summary: The partial document outlines guidelines for reviewing and expanding a pilot program, followed by preparation for full implementation and engineering development. It specifies a weekly review template assessing pilot lead metrics such as completeness, confidence, errors, and proposal-readiness. It defines clear expansion criteria—stability in discovery quality, reduced duplication, user trust, and data reliability—before advancing to successive phases with identified candidate projects. The document also lists the next recommended documents needed to transition from planning to active execution, including prototypes, engineering breakdowns, data migration strategies, and operating procedures. Finally, it introduces the engineering task breakdown, describing the purpose, guiding principles, and initial workstreams required to translate the specification into actionable engineering tasks, emphasizing structured sequencing and quality foundations.

nodes.title: Recommended Ownership Model
nodes.summary: The partial document outlines guidance for reviewing a sales pilot program and transitioning into later development phases. It includes a detailed weekly pilot review template with performance metrics such as lead processing, scoring quality, escalations, overrides, and classification issues. Expansion criteria for moving to subsequent phases emphasize stability in discovery quality, user trust, and system reliability. Candidate expansions for Phases 2 and 3 introduce advanced workflow automation and voice-based prospecting capabilities. The document also specifies the next steps in documentation—wireframes, engineering breakdowns, data migration, and operating procedures—to shift from planning to execution. Finally, it introduces an engineering task breakdown section aimed at organizing build efforts across teams, ensuring proper sequencing and modular testability, and prioritizing foundational platform development before UI features.

nodes.title: Recommended Next Document
nodes.summary: The partial document outlines a framework for reviewing and expanding a pilot program, defining ownership responsibilities, and transitioning into the engineering execution phase. It specifies a weekly pilot review template covering metrics like lead processing, scoring, completeness, and issue trends. Expansion criteria after the pilot focus on trust, reliability, and quality stability, with phased expansion plans detailing new capabilities for each stage. It also lists recommended follow-up documents such as design prototypes, engineering breakdowns, and SOPs to move from planning to implementation. The engineering section introduces workstream design principles emphasizing core logic development before UI work, modular testing, and strong data foundations, starting with the platform foundation as Workstream 1.

nodes.title: Engineering Task Breakdown by Module
nodes.summary: The partial document outlines procedures and guidelines for reviewing and expanding a pilot project, transitioning into engineering execution. It includes a recommended pilot review template detailing metrics to assess pilot performance such as lead processing, scoring, escalations, and accuracy. It defines criteria for moving from pilot to broader phases, emphasizing discovery quality, system reliability, and user trust. The document lists potential expansion candidates for subsequent phases, from additional workflow integrations to more automated systems. It also references ownership models and suggests follow-up documentation like design prototypes, engineering breakdowns, data migration plans, and operational procedures to move from planning to implementation. The final section introduces the engineering task breakdown, focusing on translating specifications into workstreams, defining design principles, and emphasizing the importance of building foundational systems before user interfaces and maintaining data and logic quality.

nodes.title: Workstream Overview
nodes.summary: The partial document outlines review and expansion procedures for a pilot program, criteria for progressing to subsequent phases, and a structured transition into engineering execution. It describes a recommended weekly pilot review template emphasizing metrics like lead processing, scoring distributions, escalation counts, and proposal-readiness. Expansion criteria focus on maintaining quality, user trust, and system reliability before advancing through defined phases, which include candidate areas for expansion such as retail workflows, customer-facing tools, and automation features. It also references ownership recommendations and specifies next steps involving design prototypes, engineering work breakdowns, data migration planning, and operational SOPs. The document then transitions into an engineering task breakdown section that defines its purpose—to convert product specifications into actionable workstreams—guided by design principles emphasizing foundational builds, modular development, testability, and prioritization of core logic prior to UI.

nodes.title: Workstream 1: Platform Foundation
nodes.summary:
The partial document outlines procedures and frameworks for reviewing, expanding, and building phases of a pilot implementation for a sales or AI-driven system. It begins with a recommended pilot review template listing key metrics to evaluate each week, such as lead processing numbers, score distributions, data completeness, escalation counts, overrides, and accuracy in proposal readiness. It then defines criteria required for expansion beyond the pilot stage, focusing on quality stability, user trust, system reliability, and recommendation effectiveness. The document identifies potential expansion phases, suggesting specific features or workflows for each. It also lists follow-up documentation needed after the current implementation plan, including design prototypes, engineering breakdowns, data migration plans, and standard operating procedures.  

Subsequent sections transition into detailed engineering task breakdowns, specifying principles for sequencing development work. The document stresses building core systems before user interfaces, maintaining testability, and treating data quality as a system dependency. It introduces initial workstreams, including the Platform Foundation—covering environment setup, role-based access, auditing, data schema, storage, and observability frameworks—and the Knowledge Core Implementation—focused on building structured knowledge services, data models, APIs, scoring configurations, and version control. These sections collectively define the foundational architecture and deliverables necessary for advancing the product from planning to active development.

nodes.title: Workstream 2: Knowledge Core Implementation
nodes.summary: The partial document outlines a multi-workstream plan for building a secure, structured knowledge platform. The first workstream, Platform Foundation, focuses on establishing the foundational application infrastructure—setting up development, staging, and production environments; implementing authentication, role-based access control, and audit logging; defining the core database schema; integrating storage and event systems; and ensuring observability and API versioning. The second workstream, Knowledge Core Implementation, centers on creating the structured knowledge layer with database tables, APIs, and version control for services, sub-services, industry playbooks, discovery questions, case-study metadata, and scoring configurations. The third workstream, Retrieval and Knowledge Access Layer, aims to enable contextual information retrieval by building ingestion pipelines, defining metadata tagging, creating retrieval APIs, and implementing filtering and traceability for document access. Each workstream specifies objectives, tasks, deliverables, dependencies, and acceptance criteria to ensure secure, traceable, and versioned knowledge operations.

nodes.title: Workstream 3: Retrieval and Knowledge Access Layer
Workstream 4: Orchestration Engine
nodes.summary: The partial document outlines two key workstreams focused on building and exposing core functionalities of a lead orchestration system. Workstream 4, “Orchestration Engine,” aims to create a decision-making layer responsible for classifying leads, selecting questions, scoring, generating recommendations, and managing escalation based on confidence and readiness. It details tasks such as building classification, scoring, and recommendation engines and defines dependencies on the Knowledge Core, Retrieval Layer, and CRM mapping. Acceptance criteria specify dynamic question selection, score updates, detailed recommendation output, and proper escalation. Workstream 5, “Lead and Discovery APIs,” focuses on providing stable APIs that connect the engine to user interfaces and external integrations, starting with developing a lead intake endpoint.

nodes.title: Workstream 5: Lead and Discovery APIs
nodes.summary:
The partial document outlines three connected workstreams within a project focused on lead management and orchestration.  
Workstream 4 defines the Orchestration Engine, responsible for decision-making processes such as lead classification, question selection, scoring, confidence evaluation, recommendations, and proposal readiness. It specifies dependencies on the Knowledge Core, Retrieval Layer, and CRM mapping, with criteria ensuring dynamic updates, escalation logic, and clear recommendation outputs.  
Workstream 5 covers the creation of Lead and Discovery APIs to expose the engine’s capabilities for user interfaces and integrations. It details endpoints for lead intake, classification, discovery sessions, scoring, recommendations, and proposal readiness, emphasizing contract compliance, structured data returns, and clear validation handling.  
Workstream 6 focuses on CRM and workflow integration, pushing intelligence outputs into CRM systems. It includes logic for lead synchronization, duplicate detection, assignment, task creation, SLA tagging, workflow updates, and error handling, delivering automated synchronization and priority handling to support actionable workflows.

nodes.title: Workstream 6: CRM & Workflow Integration
nodes.summary: The partial document outlines multiple development workstreams focused on building API endpoints, CRM integration, and frontend components for a sales and review system. It includes details such as objectives, key tasks, deliverables, dependencies, and acceptance criteria for each workstream. Early sections focus on implementing various API endpoints—lead classification, discovery, answer capture, scoring, proposal-readiness, override, and outcome logging—with attention to contract compliance, validation, and error handling. Subsequent sections describe CRM and workflow integration to sync lead data, handle duplicates, manage assignments, and trigger workflows. The document also details frontend development of a Sales Copilot interface that provides dashboards, discovery assistance, scoring displays, and actionable controls for sales teams. Lastly, it introduces the Founder Review Frontend for escalation and override functionality, emphasizing clear workflows, synchronization, and structured data handling across systems.

nodes.title: Workstream 7: Sales Copilot Frontend
nodes.summary: The partial document outlines several workstreams related to CRM integration and frontend development for sales and review processes. It covers dependencies such as Lead and Discovery APIs, event schema, and CRM field mapping, along with acceptance criteria for syncing pilot leads into the CRM with proper field mapping, failure logging, and ownership routing. Workstream 7 focuses on developing the Sales Copilot Frontend, detailing objectives, key tasks, and deliverables such as building various UI components for lead intelligence, discovery assistance, scoring panels, and actionable sales features, with criteria ensuring usability and structured workflows. Workstream 8 introduces the Founder Review Frontend, aimed at creating an escalation review and override interface, starting with a review queue screen.

nodes.title: Workstream 8: Founder Review Frontend
nodes.summary:
The partial document outlines several interconnected workstreams related to a sales and knowledge management platform. It details objectives, key tasks, deliverables, dependencies, and acceptance criteria for each workstream.  

- **Lead and Discovery Dependencies** section emphasizes syncing pilot leads into the CRM with structured field mapping, proper error handling, and routing-based owner assignment.  
- **Workstream 7: Sales Copilot Frontend** focuses on creating a comprehensive internal sales interface, including dashboards, live discovery tools, scoring panels, and action buttons. It aims for seamless discovery workflows and clarity in data presentation.  
- **Workstream 8: Founder Review Frontend** defines an escalation and override interface for founders to review leads, capture override reasons, and maintain audit-ready change records.  
- **Workstream 9: Knowledge Admin Frontend** centers on an administrative console for managing service logic, question banks, scoring configurations, version controls, and publishing workflows, supported by authentication and API dependencies.  

Overall, the document describes system components and UX interfaces designed to support lead management, review processes, administrative knowledge updates, and integration through defined APIs.

nodes.title: Workstream 9: Knowledge Admin Frontend
nodes.summary: The partial document outlines multiple workstreams focused on system feature development and administrative capabilities. It includes details on building and implementing tools for founder reviews, override management, and audit tracking, specifying deliverables, dependencies, and acceptance criteria to ensure efficient review and accurate change recording. Another section covers the development of a Knowledge Admin Frontend aimed at enabling authorized administrators to manage services, questions, scoring logic, and knowledge version control independently. The document emphasizes user interface creation, data interaction workflows, and audit-friendly displays, along with integration with required APIs and role-based access controls. The final part introduces an upcoming workstream on analytics, audit, and evaluation, indicating continued focus on data integrity and performance assessment.

nodes.title: Workstream 10: Analytics, Audit, and Evaluation
nodes.summary: The partial document outlines multiple workstreams focused on building and validating components of a larger system. The first section details creation of a Founder Review Panel that enables founders to review and override escalated leads, ensuring audit-friendly tracking and reason capture for changes. The next workstream covers development of a Knowledge Admin Frontend for controlled maintenance of services, questions, scoring, and knowledge versions, supported by APIs and role-based access. Another workstream focuses on Analytics, Audit, and Evaluation, aiming to measure system quality and business impact through dashboards, audit trails, and model traceability. The final workstream describes QA, Testing, and Evaluation Harness tasks for validating system reliability using scenario-based, regression, and acceptance tests. Overall, the document emphasizes building tools for review, administration, analytics, and testing, all with clear deliverables, dependencies, and acceptance criteria.

nodes.title: Workstream 11: QA, Testing, and Evaluation Harness
nodes.summary: The partial document outlines objectives, key tasks, deliverables, dependencies, and acceptance criteria for different workstreams and development phases of a system project. It focuses on measuring system quality, monitoring business impact, and ensuring QA, testing, and evaluation readiness. Key activities include building analytics tools (for lead quality, overrides, discovery completeness, question quality, conversion tracking, and audit trails), implementing model traceability, and developing dashboards for quality and impact monitoring. The QA and testing workstream covers creating scenario-based test libraries, defining expected-answer and routing test sets, conducting regression testing, and establishing evaluation processes before pilot expansion. The document also provides a suggested sprint-level build sequence from foundational platform setup through core APIs, orchestration, scoring, lead management, and finally the Sales Copilot UI, sequencing the project’s technical development and validation efforts.

nodes.title: Suggested Sprint-Level Build Sequence
nodes.summary:
The partial document outlines the testing, development, resourcing, and risk management plans for a project called AIM. It begins with a regression test suite and pilot triage template, focusing on dependencies across major modules and acceptance criteria to ensure core scenarios pass before expanding the live pilot. A detailed sprint-level build sequence (Sprints A–H) follows, defining incremental development phases: setting up the platform foundation, core APIs, orchestration functions, scoring and recommendation engines, CRM integrations, UI components for Sales Copilot and Founder Review, and finally QA, pilot support, and tuning. 

The resourcing section specifies a minimal viable product team composition, including core engineering, QA, and product roles, with optional roles for AI and data analysis support. The build risk notes identify potential risks such as weak knowledge quality, overly complex orchestration, CRM mismatches, and UI performance issues, with corresponding mitigations focused on narrow pilot scope, observability, and maintaining human override visibility. The section concludes with an introduction to the Knowledge Ingestion Plan, describing how to transform founder knowledge, case studies, and past materials into structured “Entity Brain” knowledge for the system.

nodes.title: Resourcing Recommendation
nodes.summary: The partial document outlines project planning and development details for a system called "Live Discovery Assistant." It covers Sprint G and Sprint H activities such as UI review, flow overrides, analytics setup, hardening, QA, pilot support, and logic tuning. It includes a "Resourcing Recommendation" specifying roles required for a minimum viable product team and optional roles for enhanced performance. The "Build Risk Notes" section identifies likely technical and operational risks with suggested mitigation strategies focusing on scope control, logging, real-scenario tuning, and human override visibility. Finally, the "Knowledge Ingestion Plan" describes how the system should transform existing founder knowledge, case studies, and service data into structured, usable knowledge for an Entity Brain system.

nodes.title: Build Risk Notes
nodes.summary: The partial document outlines project sprints, team composition, risks, and knowledge management plans for a “Live Discovery Assistant” initiative. It details upcoming sprint goals such as UI review, override flow setup, QA, and pilot support. A recommended minimum team structure is provided, listing essential and optional roles, including engineers, QA, knowledge operations, and AI specialists. The build risk section identifies potential problem areas like weak knowledge quality, premature complexity, and workflow mismatches, along with mitigation strategies emphasizing narrow pilot focus, observability, and human oversight. Finally, it introduces a knowledge ingestion plan aimed at converting founder expertise, case studies, and service assets into structured, usable organizational knowledge.

nodes.title: Knowledge Ingestion Plan
nodes.summary: The partial document outlines development and resource planning for a system called “Live Discovery Assistant.” It details sprint phases (G and H) focusing on UI review, override flow, analytics basics, QA, pilot support, and logic tuning. It specifies a recommended team structure, listing essential roles such as product owner, engineers, QA, and knowledge operations, plus optional roles like AI specialists and data analysts. The document identifies build risks—such as weak initial knowledge, complex orchestration, CRM mismatches, and UI usability issues—and suggests mitigations including narrow pilot scope, strong observability, weekly tuning, and visible human overrides. It then presents a “Knowledge Ingestion Plan,” describing how founder and organizational knowledge should be systematically converted into structured, usable intelligence. Core principles include structuring information, maintaining provenance, and distinguishing evergreen from temporary data. It further categorizes source types—structured, semi-structured, unstructured, and operational—with examples like pricing frameworks, proposals, voice notes, and SOPs. The focus is on transforming diverse inputs into validated and actionable knowledge entities.

nodes.title: Knowledge Source Categories
nodes.summary: The partial document outlines guidelines for creating structured, usable decision intelligence from various organizational knowledge sources. It emphasizes that raw documents should not be treated as the final knowledge base and introduces key ingestion principles such as normalization of critical knowledge, preserving provenance, separating durable logic from temporary data, and validating structured outputs through founder review. It categorizes knowledge sources into structured, semi-structured, unstructured, and operational types, providing examples for each. The document specifies which knowledge elements should be structured first—focusing on service definitions, discovery questions, pricing and feasibility inputs, scoring and routing rules, high-value case studies, and common objections. It also describes the ingestion pipeline stages—Inventory, Prioritization, and Extraction—detailing tasks, priorities, and expected outputs at each stage to systematize the transformation of raw organizational knowledge into structured, actionable intelligence.

nodes.title: What Must Be Structured First
nodes.summary: The partial document outlines structured ingestion processes and priorities related to staffing and operational assumptions. It emphasizes the need to organize and prioritize knowledge assets effectively before large-scale data import. Key sections include initial structuring priorities—such as focusing on services, discovery questions, critical inputs, scoring logic, routing rules, and case studies—and detailed descriptions of ingestion pipeline stages. The stages cover inventory creation (cataloging all knowledge assets and tagging them by freshness and relevance), prioritization (determining which materials to process first based on commercial utility and frequency of use), and extraction (pulling out service definitions, essential questions, pricing factors, and feasibility dependencies). The document’s main focus is on creating a systematic, value-driven approach to knowledge ingestion and management.

nodes.title: Ingestion Pipeline Stages
nodes.summary: The partial document outlines the structured process for ingesting and organizing organizational knowledge assets. It begins with key priorities for the initial ingestion wave, emphasizing the importance of structuring critical elements such as service definitions, discovery questions, quoting inputs, feasibility factors, scoring logic, routing rules, key case studies, and effective objection handling. It then details a six-stage ingestion pipeline: inventory (collecting and classifying sources), prioritization (determining intake order based on commercial relevance), extraction (identifying important business facts and patterns), normalization (converting insights into standardized data types), validation (founder or leadership review for accuracy and relevance), and publication (deploying approved structured knowledge). Lastly, it introduces a knowledge inventory template and its recommended columns for organizing source information.

nodes.title: Knowledge Inventory Template
nodes.summary: The partial document outlines a structured process for capturing, organizing, validating, and publishing institutional and founder knowledge related to services and sales. It details stages from extracting insights—such as misfit cases, objections, scoring signals—to normalizing them into structured records (service, question bank, case-study, scoring configurations, objections, proposals, feasibility notes). Validation includes commercial accuracy and completeness checks by founders or sales heads. It also specifies knowledge inventory templates with recommended columns and status values for tracking data through review stages. Additionally, it describes a founder knowledge capture plan emphasizing the documentation of founder reasoning via interviews and workshops, translating qualitative insights into structured rules and question banks. Lastly, it provides a service-by-service ingestion template outlining necessary data points for each service, including use cases, industries, inputs, objections, pitch angles, case studies, risks, and alternatives.

nodes.title: Founder Knowledge Capture Plan
nodes.summary: The partial document outlines data management and knowledge capture procedures for services within AIM. It begins with metadata fields such as source type, owner, service and industry tags, and document status, including a list of recommended status values (from Not Reviewed to Archived). It then presents a “Founder Knowledge Capture Plan,” emphasizing the importance of documenting founder reasoning through structured interviews, workshops, debriefs, and proposal reviews. The plan specifies key topics to explore with founders to codify decision-making into reusable rules and question banks. Finally, the “Service-by-Service Ingestion Template” section lists standardized information to gather for each service, including descriptions, use cases, target audiences, pricing factors, risks, and supporting materials, to enable consistent data capture and analysis.

nodes.title: Service-by-Service Ingestion Template
nodes.summary: The partial document defines structured guidelines and templates for capturing, ingesting, and standardizing key organizational knowledge related to services, founder insights, proposals, case studies, and sales patterns. It begins with metadata fields and recommended status values for document processing, outlining stages from “Not Reviewed” to “Archived.” It then details a “Founder Knowledge Capture Plan,” emphasizing extraction of founder reasoning through interviews, workshops, and debriefs to build rule sets and question banks. Following this, the “Service-by-Service Ingestion Template” specifies all essential data points to record for each service, including use cases, pricing factors, risks, and substitutes. The “Case Study Ingestion Template” focuses on capturing structured metadata instead of just storing PDFs, ensuring case studies are searchable and reusable. The “Proposal and SOW Ingestion Guidance” section explains how to selectively extract useful patterns (e.g., pricing, scope, deliverables) while avoiding reuse of outdated or client-specific content. Finally, the “Objection and Pitch-Angle Ingestion Plan” describes how to catalog objections, effective responses, and proof points by service and industry, supporting refined sales and positioning approaches. Overall, the text outlines a comprehensive framework for building a structured, tag-based knowledge base supporting service delivery, sales enablement, and organizational learning.

nodes.title: Case Study Ingestion Template
nodes.summary: The partial document outlines templates and guidance for structured data ingestion related to service activation and sales enablement. It covers three main sections: a Case Study Ingestion Template detailing required metadata such as client category, objectives, outcomes, and lessons learned; Proposal and SOW Ingestion Guidance emphasizing extraction of reusable patterns while avoiding outdated or client-specific elements; and an Objection and Pitch-Angle Ingestion Plan focused on capturing and structuring sales objections, effective responses, proof points, and strategic repositioning tips. Overall, the document stresses the importance of standardized metadata creation, selective data extraction, and organized tagging to support informed, scalable service and sales operations.

nodes.title: Proposal and SOW Ingestion Guidance
nodes.summary: The partial document outlines standardized templates and guidelines for ingesting key business information into an organized system before activating services. It covers three major areas: (1) the Case Study Ingestion Template, which defines a structured format for capturing detailed case study data including identifiers, objectives, execution summaries, outcomes, and sales relevance; (2) Proposal and Statement of Work (SOW) Ingestion Guidance, emphasizing selective extraction of reusable patterns while avoiding outdated or client-specific content; and (3) the Objection and Pitch-Angle Ingestion Plan, detailing how to structure knowledge around common sales objections, effective responses, proof points, and strategic positioning. The overall focus is on creating metadata, structured tags, and reusable insights to improve consistency, data organization, and sales effectiveness.

nodes.title: Objection and Pitch-Angle Ingestion Plan
nodes.summary:
The partial document outlines structured guidance for ingesting various types of organizational knowledge into a system referred to as the “Entity Brain.” It covers standardized templates and best practices for capturing, structuring, and tagging information for different content categories. Key sections include:  
- **Case Study Ingestion**: Guidance on recording key metadata for case studies such as client type, industry, objectives, results, and lessons learned, with an emphasis on structured data over unstructured files like PDFs.  
- **Proposal and SOW Ingestion**: Instructions for selectively extracting reusable elements (e.g., scope patterns, pricing drivers, deliverables) while avoiding outdated or client-specific details that could distort current practices.  
- **Objection and Pitch-Angle Ingestion**: Framework for mapping common objections by service or industry, associated counterarguments, proof points, and sales repositioning strategies.  
- **Ops and Feasibility Knowledge Ingestion**: Focused on incorporating operational realities such as permission dependencies, production constraints, and city-specific considerations to maintain realistic sales promises.  
- **Ingestion Sequence**: A phased rollout plan prioritizing different knowledge waves—from pilot-critical operational cases to expansion and advanced learning content—to ensure systematic knowledge integration.

nodes.title: Ops and Feasibility Knowledge Ingestion
nodes.summary: The partial document outlines key objections related to attribution, trust, and credibility, followed by a detailed section on operational and feasibility knowledge ingestion. It emphasizes capturing dependencies and constraints—such as permissions, lead-time norms, manpower, production, and city-specific factors—to ensure sales logic remains realistic and well-informed. The document explains the importance of this process for preventing over-promising or under-warning in sales efforts. It then presents a suggested sequence for knowledge ingestion under an AIM framework, divided into three waves: Wave 1 includes pilot-critical knowledge like product sampling across various touchpoints and related case studies; Wave 2 focuses on expansion knowledge covering branding and activation strategies; and Wave 3 centers on advanced learning through analysis of call transcripts and win/loss documentation.

nodes.title: Suggested Ingestion Sequence for AIM
nodes.summary: The partial document outlines a structured framework for ingesting and managing organizational knowledge related to sales, operational feasibility, and service deployment. It covers approaches to handle attribution and credibility objections, ensuring collected insights remain realistic and reliable. Key sections describe the process for capturing critical operational dependencies and constraints to prevent overpromising in sales. The document details a multi-wave ingestion sequence: starting with pilot-critical data, expanding to broader service knowledge, and advancing to learning derived from real interactions such as call transcripts and performance notes. Roles, quality checks, and validation steps are emphasized to guarantee accuracy and applicability of ingested data. It also defines policies for maintaining knowledge freshness through scheduled reviews and labeling outdated information. Finally, a first 30-day plan is provided, outlining weekly tasks for inventory building, template creation, extraction, validation, and initial publication of a verified knowledge pack.

nodes.title: Ingestion Roles and Ownership
nodes.summary: The partial document focuses on procedures and standards for knowledge ingestion and quality management.
